# Learning Contract

## еҺҹе§Ӣиҫ“е…Ҙ

ж–Үд»¶пјҡinput/questions/question-source-agent-era-260511-154949.md
й—®йўҳпјҡеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲдҪңз”Ё

## й—®йўҳзұ»еһӢ

жҳҺзЎ®й—®йўҳ

## еӯҰд№ иҖ…иғҢжҷҜдёҺеә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜ

жңӘжҢҮе®ҡпјӣжҢүйҖҡз”ЁеӯҰд№ иҖ…еӨ„зҗҶгҖӮ

## жңҖз»ҲеӯҰд№ зӣ®ж Ү

еӯҰе®ҢеҗҺеә”иғҪпјҡ
- зҗҶи§ЈеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒдёҺAI AgentдәӨдә’зҡ„ж ёеҝғжңәеҲ¶
- еҲҶжһҗеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒдҪңдёәAgentеҹәзЎҖи®ҫж–Ҫзҡ„дҪңз”Ё
- еҲӨж–ӯеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁдёҚеҗҢAgentеә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜдёӯзҡ„и§’иүІ
- еҪўжҲҗеҜ№"crypto + agent"иҝҷдёҖдәӨеҸүйўҶеҹҹзҡ„зі»з»ҹи®ӨзҹҘжЎҶжһ¶

## еҝ…йЎ»еӣһзӯ”зҡ„й—®йўҳ

1. AI Agentзҡ„еҹәжң¬е®ҡд№үе’ҢжҠҖжңҜж ҲжҳҜд»Җд№ҲпјҲдёҺеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒзӣёе…ізҡ„йғЁеҲҶпјүпјҹ
2. еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒзҡ„ж ёеҝғеҠҹиғҪпјҲж”Ҝд»ҳгҖҒиө„дә§гҖҒжҝҖеҠұгҖҒеӯҳеӮЁпјүеҰӮдҪ•дёҺAgentдәӨдә’пјҹ
3. Agentж—¶д»ЈеҜ№еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒжҸҗеҮәдәҶе“Әдәӣж–°йңҖжұӮпјҹ
4. зҺ°жңүзҡ„crypto+agentйЎ№зӣ®жңүе“ӘдәӣпјҢеҗ„и§ЈеҶідәҶд»Җд№Ҳй—®йўҳпјҹ
5. иҝҷдёҖдәӨеҸүйўҶеҹҹзҡ„ж ёеҝғжңәйҒҮе’ҢйЈҺйҷ©жҳҜд»Җд№Ҳпјҹ

## еҝ…йЎ»иҰҶзӣ–зҡ„ж ёеҝғжҰӮеҝө

1. AI Agentеҹәжң¬жһ¶жһ„пјҲж„ҹзҹҘгҖҒжҺЁзҗҶгҖҒиЎҢеҠЁгҖҒзҺҜеўғдәӨдә’пјү
2. еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеҹәзЎҖпјҲй“ҫдёҠиө„дә§гҖҒй’ұеҢ…гҖҒж”Ҝд»ҳзҪ‘з»ңгҖҒжҷәиғҪеҗҲзәҰпјү
3. AgentиғҪеҠӣжү©еұ•пјҡcryptoдҪңдёәж”Ҝд»ҳгҖҒжҝҖеҠұгҖҒиә«д»ҪгҖҒеӯҳеӮЁеұӮ
4. иҮӘдё»з»ҸжөҺд»ЈзҗҶпјҲAutonomous Economic AgentпјүжҰӮеҝө
5. зҺ°жңүcrypto+agentйЎ№зӣ®з”ҹжҖҒжҰӮи§Ҳ

## жҡӮдёҚиҰҶзӣ–

1. е…·дҪ“еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒзҡ„д»·ж јиө°еҠҝжҲ–жҠ•иө„е»әи®®
2. иҜҰз»Ҷзҡ„й“ҫдёҠеҚҸи®®е®һзҺ°з»ҶиҠӮ
3. йқһеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒзӣёе…ізҡ„AI AgentејҖеҸ‘жҠҖжңҜпјҲеҰӮRAGгҖҒFine-tuningпјү
4. еҗҲи§„е’Ңжі•еҫӢеҲҶжһҗ

## еӣәе®ҡд»»еҠЎеҚ•е…ғ

- Task 1: Framing вҖ” question-brief.md + domain-map.md
- Task 2: Mastery Path вҖ” learning-path.md + exercises.md + checkpoints.md
- Task 3: Application & Transfer вҖ” application-plan.md + transfer-plan.md

## еҗҲж јж ҮеҮҶ

1. й—®йўҳиҫ№з•Ңжё…жҷ°гҖӮ
2. йўҶеҹҹдё»е№ІдёҚйҒ—жјҸгҖӮ
3. еӯҰд№ и·Ҝеҫ„жңүйҳ¶ж®өгҖҒжңүйЎәеәҸгҖҒжңүж—¶й—ҙж„ҹгҖӮ
4. з»ғд№ е…·дҪ“еҸҜеҒҡгҖӮ
5. checkpoint еҸҜйӘҢиҜҒгҖӮ
6. еә”з”ЁдёҺиҝҒз§»и®ЎеҲ’иҙҙеҗҲиҫ“е…ҘдёӯжҳҺзЎ®зҡ„зӣ®ж ҮжҲ–еңәжҷҜпјӣиӢҘжңӘжҢҮе®ҡпјҢдҝқжҢҒйҖҡз”Ёдё”еҸҜжү§иЎҢгҖӮ
7. дёҚеј•е…Ҙиҫ“е…ҘдёӯжңӘжҸҗдҫӣзҡ„дёӘдәәгҖҒиЎҢдёҡгҖҒиҒҢдёҡжҲ–дә§е“ҒиғҢжҷҜгҖӮ

# Learning Design Guide

## Task 1 вҖ” Framing

### и®ӨзҹҘзӣ®ж Ү
её®еҠ©еӯҰд№ иҖ…жҳҺзЎ®"еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁAgentж—¶д»Јдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲдҪңз”Ё"иҝҷдёӘй—®йўҳзҡ„иҫ№з•ҢпјҢе»әз«ӢйўҶеҹҹдё»е№Іең°еӣҫгҖӮ

### еҝ…йЎ»дә§еҮә
- question-brief.md
- domain-map.md

### еҶ…е®№иҰҒжұӮ

**question-brief.md еҝ…йЎ»еҢ…еҗ«пјҡ**
- еҺҹе§Ӣй—®йўҳйҮҚиҝ°
- еӯҰд№ зӣ®ж ҮпјҲд»ҺcontractжҸҗеҸ–пјү
- жҳҺзЎ®иҫ№з•Ңе’Ңйқһзӣ®ж Ү
- еүҚзҪ®зҹҘиҜҶиҰҒжұӮ
- еёёи§ҒиҜҜеҢә

**domain-map.md еҝ…йЎ»еҢ…еҗ«пјҡ**
- дё»е№ІпјҡAI Agentеҹәжң¬жһ¶жһ„гҖҒеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒж ёеҝғеҠҹиғҪгҖҒдёӨиҖ…зҡ„дәӨеҸүзӮ№
- еҲҶж”Ҝпјҡе…·дҪ“еә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜгҖҒе·ІжңүйЎ№зӣ®еҲҶзұ»
- иҫ№зјҳиҜқйўҳпјҡжҡӮдёҚиҰҶзӣ–зҡ„йўҶеҹҹ
- еүҚзҪ®зҹҘиҜҶпјҡеҝ…йЎ»зҗҶи§Јзҡ„жҰӮеҝө
- еёёи§ҒиҜҜеҢәпјҡеӯҰд№ иҖ…е®№жҳ“ж··ж·Ҷзҡ„ең°ж–№

### дёӘжҖ§еҢ–иҫ№з•Ң
еҸӘиғҪдҪҝз”Ё learning-contract дёӯжҳҫејҸи®°еҪ•зҡ„иғҢжҷҜпјҲжңӘжҢҮе®ҡпјҢжҢүйҖҡз”ЁеӯҰд№ иҖ…еӨ„зҗҶпјүгҖӮдёҚеҫ—еј•е…Ҙд»»дҪ•зү№е®ҡиЎҢдёҡгҖҒиҒҢдёҡжҲ–еә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜзҡ„еҒҮи®ҫгҖӮ

### иҙЁйҮҸйҮҚзӮ№
domain-map еҝ…йЎ»еҢәеҲҶдё»е№І/еҲҶж”Ҝ/иҫ№зјҳпјҢиҖҢдёҚжҳҜе Ҷз Ңзӣ®еҪ•гҖӮquestion-brief зҡ„иҫ№з•Ңе’Ңйқһзӣ®ж ҮиҰҒе…·дҪ“гҖҒеҸҜж“ҚдҪңгҖӮ

---

## Task 2 вҖ” Mastery Path

### и®ӨзҹҘзӣ®ж Ү
её®еҠ©еӯҰд№ иҖ…дёҖжӯҘжӯҘжҺҢжҸЎcryptoеңЁagentж—¶д»Јзҡ„ж ёеҝғдҪңз”ЁжңәеҲ¶пјҢеҪўжҲҗеҸҜйӘҢиҜҒзҡ„зҗҶи§ЈгҖӮ

### еҝ…йЎ»дә§еҮә
- learning-path.md
- exercises.md
- checkpoints.md

### еҶ…е®№иҰҒжұӮ

**learning-path.md еҝ…йЎ»еҢ…еҗ«пјҡ**
- йҳ¶ж®өеҢ–и·Ҝеҫ„пјҲStage 1-4пјү
- жҜҸйҳ¶ж®өзӣ®ж ҮгҖҒе»әи®®иҖ—ж—¶гҖҒеӯҰд№ еҶ…е®№гҖҒдёҚеӯҰд»Җд№ҲгҖҒиҫ“еҮә/з»ғд№ гҖҒе®ҢжҲҗж ҮеҮҶ

**exercises.md еҝ…йЎ»еҢ…еҗ«пјҡ**
- иҮіе°‘4дёӘе…·дҪ“еҸҜеҒҡзҡ„з»ғд№ 
- йҒҝе…Қз©әжіӣжҸҸиҝ°
- жҜҸз»ғд№ еҝ…йЎ»жңүжҳҺзЎ®зҡ„ж“ҚдҪңжҢҮеј•е’Ңйў„жңҹиҫ“еҮә

**checkpoints.md еҝ…йЎ»еҢ…еҗ«пјҡ**

### дёӘжҖ§еҢ–иҫ№з•Ң
еҸӘиғҪдҪҝз”Ё learning-contract дёӯжҳҫејҸи®°еҪ•зҡ„иғҢжҷҜгҖӮз»ғд№ еңәжҷҜдҝқжҢҒйҖҡз”ЁгҖӮ

### иҙЁйҮҸйҮҚзӮ№

её®еҠ©еӯҰд№ иҖ…е°Ҷcrypto+agentзҡ„зҗҶи§ЈиҪ¬еҢ–дёәиЎҢеҠЁгҖҒеҲӨж–ӯжҲ–еҸҜи®Іи§ЈеҶ…е®№гҖӮ

# Project Lessons

жң¬ж–Үд»¶и®°еҪ•еҪ“еүҚ Question-to-Mastery йЎ№зӣ®дёӯеҸҜиҝҒз§»зҡ„з»ҸйӘҢгҖӮеҸӘи®°еҪ•еҜ№еҗҺз»ӯеӯҰд№ и·Ҝеҫ„з”ҹжҲҗжңүеё®еҠ©зҡ„и§„еҲҷгҖӮ

## еҪ“еүҚйЎ№зӣ®з»ҸйӘҢ

### й—®йўҳзұ»еһӢпјҡжҳҺзЎ®й—®йўҳ

еҺҹе§Ӣй—®йўҳ"еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲдҪңз”Ё"жҳҜдёҖдёӘе…ёеһӢзҡ„жҳҺзЎ®й—®йўҳпјҢеӨ„зҗҶйҮҚзӮ№жҳҜпјҡ
- жҫ„жё…й—®йўҳиҫ№з•Ң
- жӢҶеҮәеҝ…йЎ»еӣһзӯ”зҡ„еӯҗй—®йўҳ
- е»әз«Ӣеӣһзӯ”иҝҷдёӘй—®йўҳжүҖйңҖзҡ„жҰӮеҝөең°еӣҫ

### дёӘжҖ§еҢ–еҺҹеҲҷжү§иЎҢ

жң¬йЎ№зӣ®иҫ“е…ҘжңӘжҢҮе®ҡеӯҰд№ иҖ…иғҢжҷҜпјҢеӣ жӯӨпјҡ
- жүҖжңүдә§зү©жҢүйҖҡз”ЁеӯҰд№ иҖ…и®ҫи®Ў
- дёҚеј•е…Ҙд»»дҪ•зү№е®ҡиЎҢдёҡгҖҒиҒҢдёҡжҲ–дә§е“ҒеңәжҷҜ
- еә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜдҝқжҢҒйҖҡз”Ё

### йўҶеҹҹи·ЁеәҰеӨ„зҗҶ

crypto + agent жҳҜдёҖдёӘи·ЁйўҶеҹҹй—®йўҳпјҢж¶үеҸҠпјҡ
- AI/AgentжҠҖжңҜж Ҳ
- еҢәеқ—й“ҫ/еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеҹәзЎҖ
- дёӨиҖ…дәӨеҸүзҡ„жңәеҲ¶

йңҖиҰҒжҳҺзЎ®еҢәеҲҶе“ӘдәӣжҳҜдё»е№ІжҰӮеҝөпјҢе“ӘдәӣжҳҜеҲҶж”ҜжҲ–иҫ№зјҳиҜқйўҳпјҢйҒҝе…ҚеӯҰд№ и·Ҝеҫ„иҝҮдәҺеҸ‘ж•ЈгҖӮ

input.content:
# Question Brief

## Original Question

еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲдҪңз”Ё

## Learning Objectives

After completing this learning path, you will be able to:

- Understand the core mechanisms of interaction between cryptocurrency and AI Agents
- Analyze the role of cryptocurrency as Agent infrastructure
- Judge the role of cryptocurrency in different Agent application scenarios
- Form a systematic cognitive framework for the "crypto + agent" intersection

## Boundaries and Non-Objectives

### What You Will Learn

- AI Agent basic architecture (perception, reasoning, action, environment interaction)
- Cryptocurrency core functions (on-chain assets, wallets, payment networks, smart contracts)
- How crypto extends Agent capabilities (as payment, incentive, identity, storage layer)
- Autonomous Economic Agent (AEA) concept
- Overview of existing crypto+agent project ecosystem

### What You Will NOT Learn

- Specific cryptocurrency price trends or investment advice
- Detailed on-chain protocol implementation details
- Non-crypto AI Agent development technologies (RAG, Fine-tuning)
- Compliance and legal analysis

## Prerequisites

- Basic understanding of what AI is (broad concept, not deep technical knowledge)
- Basic understanding of what cryptocurrency is (broad concept)
- No prior knowledge of AI Agent architecture required
- No prior knowledge of crypto+agent intersection required

## Common Misconceptions

1. **"Crypto is just a payment method for AI"** вҖ” In the Agent era, crypto serves multiple roles: payment, incentive mechanism, identity layer, stake mechanism, and long-term memory storage.

2. **"AI Agents automatically understand blockchain"** вҖ” There is a significant technical gap between traditional AI systems and blockchain interaction. Agents need specific tools and interfaces to operate on-chain.

3. **"Crypto+agent is only about financial agents"** вҖ” Beyond trading bots, crypto+agent covers identity, coordination, governance, and infrastructure roles.

4. **"Agents will replace all crypto applications"** вҖ” Agents are a new interface layer; crypto protocols remain the underlying infrastructure with their own logic and tradeoffs.

5. **"The technology is already mature and production-ready"** вҖ” The crypto+agent space is still early-stage; many projects are experimental and infrastructure is rapidly evolving.

# Domain Map: Crypto + Agent Intersection

## Core Concept Relationship

```
AI Agent (perceives, reasons, acts)
       в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ needs capabilities
       в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Crypto Layer (as infrastructure)        в”Ӯ
в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
в”Ӯ вҖў Payment/settlement                    в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ вҖў Incentive/stake                       в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ вҖў Identity/authentication                в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ вҖў Storage/memory                        в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ вҖў Coordination/computation               в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

---

## 1. Main Trunk Concepts

### 1.1 AI Agent Architecture

| Component | Role | Crypto Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Perception | Receives external input (APIs, user requests, environment data) | Can receive on-chain events as input |
| Reasoning | Processes information, makes decisions | May consider crypto incentives in decisions |
| Action | Executes tasks | Can trigger on-chain transactions |
| Environment | Interacts with external systems | Blockchain as environment |

### 1.2 Cryptocurrency Core Functions

| Function | Description | Agent Application |
|---|---|---|
| On-chain Assets | Tokens, NFTs, digital property | Agent holds and manages assets |
| Wallets | Cryptographic key management | Agent identity and ownership |
| Payment Network | Value transfer settlement | Agent receives compensation, pays for services |
| Smart Contracts | Programmable logic execution | Agent runs autonomous code |
| Staking/Locking | Economic commitment | Agent stakes to secure operations, earn rewards |

### 1.3 Agent Capability Extensions via Crypto

- **Payment Layer**: Agents pay for services, receive compensation, settle transactions
- **Incentive Layer**: Token-based reward systems motivate agent behavior
- **Identity Layer**: Wallet-based identity, reputation tied to on-chain history
- **Storage Layer**: On-chain state, IPFS, and decentralized storage for agent memory
- **Coordination Layer**: Agents coordinate via smart contracts without centralized intermediaries

---

## 2. Branch Topics

### 2.1 Application Scenarios

- **Autonomous Economic Agents (AEA)**: Agents that earn, spend, and invest independently
- **Decentralized AI Services**: Agent networks powered by crypto incentives
- **On-chain Reasoning**: Agents that analyze and act on blockchain data
- **Token-gated Access**: Agents unlock services via crypto stakes or holdings

### 2.2 Existing Project Categories

| Category | Example Function |
|---|---|
| Agent Frameworks with Crypto | Protocol-level agent toolkits |
| DeFi Agent Strategies | Automated trading, yield optimization |
| AI + Blockchain Infrastructure | Compute networks, data availability |
| Autonomous Agent Platforms | Agent-to-agent coordination protocols |

---

## 3. Edge Topics (Not Covered in This Learning Path)

- Deep technical implementation of specific consensus mechanisms
- Cryptocurrency trading strategies and market analysis
- Legal and regulatory compliance frameworks
- Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning techniques
- ZK-proof technical details

---

## 4. Prerequisites Map

```
Level 1: Basic Awareness
в”ңв”Җв”Җ What is AI (broad concept)
в””в”Җв”Җ What is Cryptocurrency (broad concept)

Level 2: Foundational Understanding
в”ңв”Җв”Җ How AI Agents work (basic architecture)
в””в”Җв”Җ How blockchain/crypto works (basic functions)

Level 3: Intersection Understanding
в”ңв”Җв”Җ Why crypto is relevant to agents
в””в”Җв”Җ What problems does crypto solve for agents
```

---

## 5. Common Misconceptions Map

| Misconception | Reality |
|---|---|
| "Crypto is just digital payment" | Crypto provides payment, identity, storage, coordination |
| "Agents can read blockchain naturally" | Agents need specific tools/interfaces to interact with chains |
| "Crypto+agent is only trading bots" | Covers identity, coordination, governance, infrastructure |
| "Agents replace crypto protocols" | Agents are interface layer; crypto remains underlying infrastructure |
| "Technology is production-ready" | Space is early-stage, experimental, rapidly evolving |

---

## 6. Key Questions Structure

This domain can be understood by answering five core questions:

1. **What are AI Agents?** вҖ” Core architecture and capabilities
2. **What is cryptocurrency's role?** вҖ” Beyond payments to infrastructure functions
3. **What new demands does Agent era place on crypto?** вҖ” New use cases and requirements
4. **What existing projects exist?** вҖ” Real examples and solutions
5. **What are opportunities and risks?** вҖ” Strategic outlook for the intersection

1	# Question Brief
2	
3	## Original Question
4	
5	еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲдҪңз”Ё
6	
7	## Learning Objectives
8	
9	After completing this learning path, you will be able to:
10	
11	- Understand the core mechanisms of interaction between cryptocurrency and AI Agents
12	- Analyze the role of cryptocurrency as Agent infrastructure
13	- Judge the role of cryptocurrency in different Agent application scenarios
14	- Form a systematic cognitive framework for the "crypto + agent" intersection
15	
16	## Boundaries and Non-Objectives
17	
18	### What You Will Learn
19	
20	- AI Agent basic architecture (perception, reasoning, action, environment interaction)
21	- Cryptocurrency core functions (on-chain assets, wallets, payment networks, smart contracts)
22	- How crypto extends Agent capabilities (as payment, incentive, identity, storage layer)
23	- Autonomous Economic Agent (AEA) concept
24	- Overview of existing crypto+agent project ecosystem
25	
26	### What You Will NOT Learn
27	
28	- Specific cryptocurrency price trends or investment advice
29	- Detailed on-chain protocol implementation details
30	- Non-crypto AI Agent development technologies (RAG, Fine-tuning)
31	- Compliance and legal analysis
32	
33	## Prerequisites
34	
35	- Basic understanding of what AI is (broad concept, not deep technical knowledge)
36	- Basic understanding of what cryptocurrency is (broad concept)
37	- No prior knowledge of AI Agent architecture required
38	- No prior knowledge of crypto+agent intersection required
39	
40	## Common Misconceptions
41	
42	1. **"Crypto is just a payment method for AI"** вҖ” In the Agent era, crypto serves multiple roles: payment, incentive mechanism, identity layer, stake mechanism, and long-term memory storage.
43	
44	2. **"AI Agents automatically understand blockchain"** вҖ” There is a significant technical gap between traditional AI systems and blockchain interaction. Agents need specific tools and interfaces to operate on-chain.
45	
46	3. **"Crypto+agent is only about financial agents"** вҖ” Beyond trading bots, crypto+agent covers identity, coordination, governance, and infrastructure roles.
47	
48	4. **"Agents will replace all crypto applications"** вҖ” Agents are a new interface layer; crypto protocols remain the underlying infrastructure with their own logic and tradeoffs.
49	
50	5. **"The technology is already mature and production-ready"** вҖ” The crypto+agent space is still early-stage; many projects are experimental and infrastructure is rapidly evolving.

### иҜ„дј°иҜҙжҳҺ

**Question Quality**пјҡеҺҹе§Ӣй—®йўҳ"еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲдҪңз”Ё"иў«еҮҶзЎ®йҮҚиҝ°дёә4дёӘе…·дҪ“еӯҰд№ зӣ®ж ҮпјҲзҗҶи§Јж ёеҝғжңәеҲ¶гҖҒеҲҶжһҗеҹәзЎҖи®ҫж–ҪдҪңз”ЁгҖҒеҲӨж–ӯдёҚеҗҢеңәжҷҜи§’иүІгҖҒеҪўжҲҗзі»з»ҹи®ӨзҹҘжЎҶжһ¶пјүгҖӮиҫ№з•Ңе’Ңйқһзӣ®ж Үжё…жҷ°еҲ—еҮәпјҲжҳҺзЎ®дёҚж¶өзӣ–д»·ж јжҠ•иө„гҖҒеҚҸи®®е®һзҺ°з»ҶиҠӮгҖҒйқһcryptoзҡ„agentжҠҖжңҜгҖҒеҗҲи§„пјүгҖӮеӯҰе®ҢеҗҺиғҪеҒҡд»Җд№ҲжҳҺзЎ®гҖӮ

**Coverage**пјҡdomain-map.md иҰҶзӣ–дәҶlearning-contractдёӯжүҖжңү5дёӘеҝ…йЎ»еӣһзӯ”зҡ„й—®йўҳпјҢдё»е№ІжҰӮеҝөе®Ңж•ҙпјҲAI Agentжһ¶жһ„гҖҒcryptoж ёеҝғеҠҹиғҪгҖҒAgentиғҪеҠӣжү©еұ•гҖҒAEAжҰӮеҝөгҖҒз”ҹжҖҒжҰӮи§ҲпјүгҖӮеҲҶж”ҜиҜқйўҳпјҲеә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜгҖҒйЎ№зӣ®зұ»еҲ«пјүе’Ңиҫ№зјҳиҜқйўҳпјҲжҺ’йҷӨйЎ№пјүеқҮжңүеҢәеҲҶгҖӮcommon misconceptions map дёҺ question-brief.md дёӯзҡ„5дёӘиҜҜи§ЈзӮ№дёҖиҮҙгҖӮеүҚзҪ®зҹҘиҜҶең°еӣҫд»ҺLevel 1еҲ°Level 3жңүеұӮж¬ЎгҖӮ

**Clarity**пјҡз»“жһ„жё…жҷ°вҖ”вҖ”domain-map.md дҪҝз”ЁеӣҫиЎЁгҖҒиЎЁж јгҖҒеұӮзә§ж Үйўҳз»„з»ҮеҶ…е®№гҖӮдё»е№І/еҲҶж”Ҝ/иҫ№зјҳиҜқйўҳз”Ёvisual diagramе’Ңж ҮйўҳеұӮзә§еҢәеҲҶгҖӮжңҜиҜӯпјҲеҰӮAEAгҖҒon-chain assetsпјүеңЁиЎЁж јдёӯз»ҷеҮәи§ЈйҮҠгҖӮ5дёӘж ёеҝғй—®йўҳз»“жһ„еҢ–е‘ҲзҺ°гҖӮжңӘжқҘеӨҚд№ ж—¶еҸҜеҝ«йҖҹе®ҡдҪҚгҖӮ

**Actionability**пјҡTask 1дёәFramingйҳ¶ж®өпјҢдә§зү©дёәй—®йўҳжҰӮиҝ°е’ҢйўҶеҹҹең°еӣҫпјҢдёҚжҳҜеӯҰд№ и·Ҝеҫ„жң¬иә«гҖӮquestion-brief.mdдёӯзҡ„"What You Will Learn"е’Ң"What You Will NOT Learn"з»ҷеҮәдәҶжё…жҷ°зҡ„иҫ№з•ҢпјҢиҝҷеңЁжң¬йҳ¶ж®өжҳҜеҗҲйҖӮзҡ„гҖӮжҡӮдёҚжүЈеҲҶпјҢд»…дҪңйў„иӯҰгҖӮ

**User Context Fit**пјҡlearning-contractжҳҺзЎ®еҶҷжҳҺ"жңӘжҢҮе®ҡпјӣжҢүйҖҡз”ЁеӯҰд№ иҖ…еӨ„зҗҶ"гҖӮдә§зү©дёӯж— дёӘдәә/иЎҢдёҡ/иҒҢдёҡ/дә§е“ҒиғҢжҷҜеј•е…ҘгҖӮAI Agentе’ҢcryptoйғҪжҳҜйҖҡз”ЁжҰӮеҝөи§ЈйҮҠпјҢжңӘз»‘е®ҡзү№е®ҡиҒҢдёҡжҲ–еә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜгҖӮ

**Transferability**пјҡdomain-map.mdз¬¬6иҠӮ"Key Questions Structure"з»ҷеҮәдәҶ5дёӘж ёеҝғй—®йўҳжЎҶжһ¶пјҢиҝҷдәӣй—®йўҳеҸҜд»Ҙеј•еҜјеҗҺз»ӯеӯҰд№ гҖҒи®Ёи®әжҲ–йЎ№зӣ®жҺўзҙўгҖӮдҪҶе…·дҪ“иҝҒз§»и®ЎеҲ’пјҲapplication-planгҖҒtransfer-planпјүеұһдәҺTask 3иҢғз•ҙпјҢTask 1дә§зү©е·ІеҒҡйҖӮеҪ“й“әеһ«гҖӮ

### дә§зү©дёҖиҮҙжҖ§жЈҖжҹҘ

- question-brief.md дёӯзҡ„"Common Misconceptions"5зӮ№дёҺdomain-map.mdз¬¬5иҠӮ"Common Misconceptions Map"иЎЁж јдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ

toolCalls.result:
1	# Question Brief
2	
3	## Original Question
4	
5	еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲдҪңз”Ё
6	
7	## Learning Objectives
8	
9	After completing this learning path, you will be able to:
10	
11	- Understand the core mechanisms of interaction between cryptocurrency and AI Agents
12	- Analyze the role of cryptocurrency as Agent infrastructure
13	- Judge the role of cryptocurrency in different Agent application scenarios
14	- Form a systematic cognitive framework for the "crypto + agent" intersection
15	
16	## Boundaries and Non-Objectives
17	
18	### What You Will Learn
19	
20	- AI Agent basic architecture (perception, reasoning, action, environment interaction)
21	- Cryptocurrency core functions (on-chain assets, wallets, payment networks, smart contracts)
22	- How crypto extends Agent capabilities (as payment, incentive, identity, storage layer)
23	- Autonomous Economic Agent (AEA) concept
24	- Overview of existing crypto+agent project ecosystem
25	
26	### What You Will NOT Learn
27	
28	- Specific cryptocurrency price trends or investment advice
29	- Detailed on-chain protocol implementation details
30	- Non-crypto AI Agent development technologies (RAG, Fine-tuning)
31	- Compliance and legal analysis
32	
33	## Prerequisites
34	
35	- Basic understanding of what AI is (broad concept, not deep technical knowledge)
36	- Basic understanding of what cryptocurrency is (broad concept)
37	- No prior knowledge of AI Agent architecture required
38	- No prior knowledge of crypto+agent intersection required
39	
40	## Common Misconceptions
41	
42	1. **"Crypto is just a payment method for AI"** вҖ” In the Agent era, crypto serves multiple roles: payment, incentive mechanism, identity layer, stake mechanism, and long-term memory storage.
43	
44	2. **"AI Agents automatically understand blockchain"** вҖ” There is a significant technical gap between traditional AI systems and blockchain interaction. Agents need specific tools and interfaces to operate on-chain.
45	
46	3. **"Crypto+agent is only about financial agents"** вҖ” Beyond trading bots, crypto+agent covers identity, coordination, governance, and infrastructure roles.
47	
48	4. **"Agents will replace all crypto applications"** вҖ” Agents are a new interface layer; crypto protocols remain the underlying infrastructure with their own logic and tradeoffs.
49	
50	5. **"The technology is already mature and production-ready"** вҖ” The crypto+agent space is still early-stage; many projects are experimental and infrastructure is rapidly evolving.

21	---
22	
23	## 1. Main Trunk Concepts
24	
25	### 1.1 AI Agent Architecture
26	
27	| Component | Role | Crypto Relevance |
28	|---|---|---|
29	| Perception | Receives external input (APIs, user requests, environment data) | Can receive on-chain events as input |
30	| Reasoning | Processes information, makes decisions | May consider crypto incentives in decisions |
31	| Action | Executes tasks | Can trigger on-chain transactions |
32	| Environment | Interacts with external systems | Blockchain as environment |
33	
34	### 1.2 Cryptocurrency Core Functions
35	
36	| Function | Description | Agent Application |
37	|---|---|---|
38	| On-chain Assets | Tokens, NFTs, digital property | Agent holds and manages assets |
39	| Wallets | Cryptographic key management | Agent identity and ownership |
40	| Payment Network | Value transfer settlement | Agent receives compensation, pays for services |
41	| Smart Contracts | Programmable logic execution | Agent runs autonomous code |
42	| Staking/Locking | Economic commitment | Agent stakes to secure operations, earn rewards |
43	
44	### 1.3 Agent Capability Extensions via Crypto
45	
46	- **Payment Layer**: Agents pay for services, receive compensation, settle transactions
47	- **Incentive Layer**: Token-based reward systems motivate agent behavior
48	- **Identity Layer**: Wallet-based identity, reputation tied to on-chain history
49	- **Storage Layer**: On-chain state, IPFS, and decentralized storage for agent memory
50	- **Coordination Layer**: Agents coordinate via smart contracts without centralized intermediaries
51

54	## 2. Branch Topics
55	
56	### 2.1 Application Scenarios
57	
58	- **Autonomous Economic Agents (AEA)**: Agents that earn, spend, and invest independently
59	- **Decentralized AI Services**: Agent networks powered by crypto incentives
60	- **On-chain Reasoning**: Agents that analyze and act on blockchain data
61	- **Token-gated Access**: Agents unlock services via crypto stakes or holdings
62	
63	### 2.2 Existing Project Categories
64	
65	| Category | Example Function |
66	|---|---|
67	| Agent Frameworks with Crypto | Protocol-level agent toolkits |
68	| DeFi Agent Strategies | Automated trading, yield optimization |
69	| AI + Blockchain Infrastructure | Compute networks, data availability |
70	| Autonomous Agent Platforms | Agent-to-agent coordination protocols |
71	
72	---
73	
74	## 3. Edge Topics (Not Covered in This Learning Path)
75	
76	- Deep technical implementation of specific consensus mechanisms
77	- Cryptocurrency trading strategies and market analysis
78	- Legal and regulatory compliance frameworks
79	- Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning techniques
80	- ZK-proof technical details
81	
82	---
83	
84	## 4. Prerequisites Map
85	
86	```
87	Level 1: Basic Awareness
88	в”ңв”Җв”Җ What is AI (broad concept)
89	в””в”Җв”Җ What is Cryptocurrency (broad concept)
90	
91	Level 2: Foundational Understanding
92	в”ңв”Җв”Җ How AI Agents work (basic architecture)
93	в””в”Җв”Җ How blockchain/crypto works (basic functions)
94	
95	Level 3: Intersection Understanding
96	в”ңв”Җв”Җ Why crypto is relevant to agents
97	в””в”Җв”Җ What problems does crypto solve for agents
98	```
99	
100	---
101	
102	## 5. Common Misconceptions Map
103	
104	| Misconception | Reality |
105	|---|---|
106	| "Crypto is just digital payment" | Crypto provides payment, identity, storage, coordination |
107	| "Agents can read blockchain naturally" | Agents need specific tools/interfaces to interact with chains |
108	| "Crypto+agent is only trading bots" | Covers identity, coordination, governance, infrastructure |
109	| "Agents replace crypto protocols" | Agents are interface layer; crypto remains underlying infrastructure |
110	| "Technology is production-ready" | Space is early-stage, experimental, rapidly evolving |
111	
112	---
113	
114	## 6. Key Questions Structure
115	
116	This domain can be understood by answering five core questions:
117	
118	1. **What are AI Agents?** вҖ” Core architecture and capabilities
119	2. **What is cryptocurrency's role?** вҖ” Beyond payments to infrastructure functions
120	3. **What new demands does Agent era place on crypto?** вҖ” New use cases and requirements
121	4. **What existing projects exist?** вҖ” Real examples and solutions
122	5. **What are opportunities and risks?** вҖ” Strategic outlook for the intersection

input.content:
# Learning Path: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era

## Overview

This learning path is structured in 4 stages, designed to take approximately 8-12 hours total for a general learner with basic knowledge of AI and cryptocurrency concepts.

---

## Stage 1: Establish Boundaries and Core Concepts

### Goal
Build a clear mental model of AI Agent architecture and cryptocurrency's core functions, without conflating the two.

### Suggested Time
2-3 hours

### Learning Content

1. **AI Agent Architecture**
   - Understand the 4-component model: Perception, Reasoning, Action, Environment
   - Recognize that Agents are systems that observe, decide, and act autonomously
   - Key insight: Agents are not just "AI that does things" вҖ” they have structured inputs and outputs

2. **Cryptocurrency Core Functions**
   - Review the 5 core functions relevant to agents: on-chain assets, wallets, payment networks, smart contracts, staking
   - Understand each function's basic mechanism (what it does, not how it is implemented technically)
   - Key insight: Crypto is not just "digital money" вҖ” it is a composable infrastructure toolkit

3. **The Gap Between Them**
   - Recognize that AI systems and blockchain systems were built independently
   - Agents cannot "natively understand" blockchain вҖ” they need interfaces

### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
- Deep technical details of how LLM reasoning works
- Consensus mechanisms or blockchain protocol internals
- Specific token economics or yield farming strategies

### Completion Criteria
- [ ] Can explain AI Agent's 4-component model in your own words
- [ ] Can list cryptocurrency's 5 core functions relevant to agents
- [ ] Can articulate why a "gap" exists between AI systems and blockchain systems

---

## Stage 2: Understand How Crypto Extends Agent Capabilities

### Goal
Move beyond crypto-as-payment to understand the full range of roles crypto plays for agents.

### Suggested Time
2-3 hours

### Learning Content

1. **Payment Layer**
   - How agents use crypto to pay for services (compute, data, API calls)
   - How agents receive compensation for their own services
   - Micropayments and programmable settlement

2. **Incentive Layer**
   - Token-based reward systems that motivate agent behavior
   - Stake-and-reward mechanisms
   - Bonding curves and economicжёёжҲҸзҡ„ Agent жҝҖеҠұи®ҫи®Ў

3. **Identity Layer**
   - Wallet-based identity: public/private keys as agent identifiers
   - On-chain reputation: history of actions as credibility
   - Verifiable credentials and proof mechanisms

4. **Storage/Memory Layer**
   - On-chain state as persistent memory
   - IPFS and decentralized storage for agent data
   - The tradeoffs: cost, latency, decentralization

5. **Coordination Layer**
   - Smart contracts as agent-to-agent agreements
   - Decentralized agent registries and directories
   - Multi-agent workflows without centralized orchestrators

### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
- How to implement specific smart contracts
- Zero-knowledge proof systems
- Detailed DeFi protocol mechanics

### Completion Criteria
- [ ] Can describe at least 3 non-payment roles crypto plays for agents
- [ ] Can explain the basic tradeoff of on-chain vs off-chain storage for agent memory
- [ ] Can give one example of how crypto coordinates multiple agents

---

## Stage 3: Examine Real Projects and Applications

### Goal
Connect abstract concepts to real-world crypto+agent projects, understanding what problems each solves.

### Suggested Time
2-3 hours

### Learning Content

1. **Autonomous Economic Agents (AEA)**
   - What they are: agents that earn, spend, invest independently
   - How they use crypto: payment receipts, expense payment, stake management
   - Real project examples and their approaches

2. **Agent Infrastructure Projects**
   - Projects that provide agents with crypto-native tools (wallets, payment rails, identity)
   - Frameworks that simplify crypto+agent integration
   - Data availability and compute networks for agents

3. **DeFi Agent Strategies**
   - Automated trading agents that operate on-chain
   - Yield optimization agents
   - Risk management agents

4. **On-chain Reasoning Agents**
   - Agents that analyze blockchain data
   - MEV capture agents
   - Governance participation agents

### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
- Specific trading strategy formulas
- How to audit smart contract security
- How to build a production agent system

### Completion Criteria
- [ ] Can categorize at least 3 real projects by their primary crypto+agent function
- [ ] Can identify which crypto capability (payment/incentive/identity/storage/coordination) each project primarily uses
- [ ] Can articulate one limitation or unsolved problem in current crypto+agent projects

---

## Stage 4: Form Your Systematic Framework

### Goal
Synthesize learning into a personal cognitive framework for evaluating crypto's role in any agent application.

### Suggested Time
1-2 hours

### Learning Content

1. **The Crypto+Agent Evaluation Matrix**
   - For any agent application, ask: Does it need crypto? Which crypto functions? What are the tradeoffs?
   - Distinguish genuine crypto utility from unnecessary crypto integration

2. **Opportunity and Risk Assessment**
   - Key opportunities: programmable value, trustless coordination, decentralized identity, economic sovereignty
   - Key risks: technical immaturity, security vulnerabilities, regulatory uncertainty, user experience gaps

3. **The 5 Questions Framework**
   For any crypto+agent application, answer:
   1. What agent capability does crypto enable here?
   2. What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?
   3. What new risks does crypto introduce?
   4. Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?
   5. What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?

### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
- How to predict market outcomes
- Legal or compliance advice for specific jurisdictions
- How to build production-grade systems

### Completion Criteria
- [ ] Can apply the 5 Questions Framework to a new crypto+agent concept
- [ ] Can distinguish genuine crypto utility from hype in a crypto+agent project
- [ ] Can articulate both the top opportunity and top risk for the crypto+agent intersection

---

## Path Summary Table

| Stage | Focus | Time | Key Output |
|-------|-------|------|------------|
| 1 | Core concepts and boundaries | 2-3h | Mental models of Agent architecture and crypto functions |
| 2 | Crypto as capability extension | 2-3h | Understanding of 5 crypto roles for agents |
| 3 | Real projects examination | 2-3h | Categorization of existing projects |
| 4 | Systematic framework formation | 1-2h | Personal 5-questions evaluation tool |

# Exercises: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era

These exercises are designed to be completed after each stage of the learning path. Each exercise is actionable and produces a concrete output.

---

## Exercise 1: Decompose an AI Agent

**Stage:** 1
**Time:** 30-45 minutes
**Format:** Written analysis

### Instructions

Select one of the following agent types (or choose your own):
- A news summarization agent
- A code review agent
- A personal finance agent
- A research assistant agent

For your chosen agent, complete the following decomposition:

1. **Perception component**: What inputs does this agent receive? List at least 3.
2. **Reasoning component**: What decisions does this agent make? List at least 3.
3. **Action component**: What outputs or actions does this agent produce? List at least 3.
4. **Environment component**: What external systems does this agent interact with?

### Expected Output

A structured document (300-500 words) that:
- Identifies all 4 components with specific examples
- Notes where blockchain/crypto systems could plausibly fit into the agent's architecture
- States one way crypto could NOT help this agent (misapplication check)

### Verification Check
- Does your decomposition identify at least 3 inputs, 3 decisions, and 3 outputs?
- Did you identify at least 1 plausible crypto application AND 1 implausible crypto application?

---

## Exercise 2: Map Crypto Functions to Agent Needs

**Stage:** 2
**Time:** 45-60 minutes
**Format:** Comparative table

### Instructions

Create a comparison table with the following structure:

| Crypto Function | Agent Need It Addresses | Without Crypto, Agent Would... | Risk/Tradeoff Introduced |
|----------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|

Fill in at least 5 rows, covering different crypto functions from:
- Payment/settlement
- Incentive/stake
- Identity/authentication
- Storage/memory
- Coordination

### Expected Output

A table with 5+ rows, each containing:
- A crypto function correctly matched to an agent need
- A description of how the agent would function without crypto
- An honest risk or tradeoff introduced by using crypto

### Verification Check
- Did you cover at least 4 of the 5 crypto functions?
- Does each row include a genuine risk/tradeoff (not just "it costs gas fees")?
- Is the "Without Crypto" column specific to an agent context?

---

## Exercise 3: Analyze a Real Crypto+Agent Project

**Stage:** 3
**Time:** 60-90 minutes
**Format:** Project analysis brief

### Instructions

Research one of the following project categories (or find a specific project on your own):
- **Category A**: Agent frameworks with crypto integration (e.g., ixo, Autonolas, or similar)
- **Category B**: DeFi agent strategies (e.g., agents that automate trading or yield strategies)
- **Category C**: Decentralized AI infrastructure (e.g., compute or data availability networks)

Write a 1-page analysis covering:

1. **Project name and category** (2-3 sentences)
2. **What the project does** (succinct description)
3. **Which crypto functions does it use?** (list from: payment, incentive, identity, storage, coordination)
4. **Which Agent capabilities does it enable?**
5. **One genuine strength** of the approach
6. **One unresolved problem or limitation**

### Expected Output

A structured brief (400-600 words) with all 6 sections clearly labeled.

### Verification Check
- Did you identify the project's primary crypto function?
- Did you identify the agent capability being enabled?
- Does your analysis include both a strength AND a limitation?
- Is the limitation specific (not just "early stage")?

---

## Exercise 4: Apply the 5-Questions Framework

**Stage:** 4
**Time:** 45-60 minutes
**Format:** Structured evaluation

### Instructions

Select a hypothetical or real crypto+agent application that has NOT been covered in the learning path. For example:
- A social media agent that earns tokens for quality content moderation
- A healthcare agent that stores patient data on-chain
- A creative agent that registers AI-generated art as NFTs and manages licensing royalties

Apply the 5-Questions Framework:

1. **What agent capability does crypto enable here?**
2. **What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?**
3. **What new risks does crypto introduce?**
4. **Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?**
5. **What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?**

### Expected Output

A structured document (400-500 words) that:
- Names and describes the application being evaluated
- Answers each of the 5 questions with specific reasoning
- Includes a final verdict: "Genuine crypto utility" / "Mixed utility" / "Unnecessary crypto integration"

### Verification Check
- Did you answer all 5 questions?
- Is the verdict supported by the answers to the preceding questions?
- Did you consider scaling implications (question 5)?

---

## Exercise 5: Identify Misconceptions in Crypto+Agent Claims

**Stage:** 4 (capstone)
**Time:** 30-45 minutes
**Format:** Correction exercise

### Instructions

Review the following claims (or find 3 claims from crypto+agent articles, social posts, or project documentation online). For each claim:

1. State whether the claim is **correct**, **partially correct**, or **misleading**
2. If misleading or partially correct, explain why in 2-3 sentences
3. Suggest a more accurate framing

### Claims to Analyze

1. "AI Agents will make cryptocurrency obsolete by replacing smart contracts with AI reasoning."
2. "Crypto wallets are just like bank accounts for agents."
3. "Any AI Agent can automatically interact with any blockchain without additional infrastructure."
4. "The main value of crypto for agents is faster payments."
5. "Once AI Agents are mainstream, crypto will be unnecessary because AI can handle all coordination."

### Expected Output

A table or list with 5 claims, each labeled and corrected.

### Verification Check
- Did you correctly identify at least 3 claims as misleading or partially correct?
- Are your corrections specific and grounded in concepts from the learning path?
- Do corrections reference specific crypto functions or agent capabilities?

---

## Exercise Summary

| # | Exercise | Stage | Time | Output |
|---|----------|-------|------|--------|
| 1 | Decompose an AI Agent | 1 | 30-45min | Written decomposition |
| 2 | Map Crypto to Agent Needs | 2 | 45-60min | Comparison table |
| 3 | Analyze a Real Project | 3 | 60-90min | Project brief |
| 4 | Apply 5-Questions Framework | 4 | 45-60min | Evaluation document |
| 5 | Identify Misconceptions | 4 | 30-45min | Correction table |

input.content:
# Checkpoints: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era

Checkpoints are verifiable milestones that confirm genuine understanding, not just familiarity with terms. Each checkpoint requires active demonstration, not passive recognition.

---

## Checkpoint 1: AI Agent Architecture and Crypto Core Functions

**Domain:** Stage 1
**Type:** Oral or written explanation
**Evaluator:** Self or peer

### Task

Explain the following to a non-technical person in 3-5 minutes:

1. What an AI Agent is (using the 4-component model: Perception, Reasoning, Action, Environment)
2. What cryptocurrency's 5 core functions are (on-chain assets, wallets, payment network, smart contracts, staking)
3. Why these two systems were historically separate and what the "integration gap" is

### Pass Criteria

- [ ] Explains all 4 agent components with at least one concrete example for each
- [ ] Lists all 5 crypto functions correctly
- [ ] Articulates the integration gap without using technical jargon
- [ ] Uses no more than 2 minutes on agent architecture and 2 minutes on crypto functions
- [ ] Answers one follow-up question about either component correctly

### Fail Indicators

- Skips or conflates agent components (e.g., "the AI does everything")
- Lists fewer than 5 crypto functions or includes non-crypto functions
- Cannot explain why agents cannot natively "understand" blockchain
- Uses jargon without explanation when speaking to non-technical audience

---

## Checkpoint 2: Crypto Roles for Agents

**Domain:** Stage 2
**Type:** Written or diagram-based demonstration
**Evaluator:** Self-check against criteria

### Task

Complete the following task without referring to source materials:

Draw or describe a scenario where a single AI Agent uses at least 3 different crypto functions simultaneously. For each function used, specify:
- Which crypto function is being used
- What problem it solves for the agent
- What the agent would do if that crypto function were unavailable

### Pass Criteria

- [ ] Describes a scenario with at least 3 distinct crypto functions
- [ ] Each function is correctly identified and matched to a genuine agent need
- [ ] For each function, the alternative (without crypto) is specific, not generic
- [ ] The scenario is coherent and internally consistent
- [ ] Storage/memory and coordination roles are not confused with payment

### Fail Indicators

- Describes a scenario that only uses crypto as payment
- Uses fewer than 3 crypto functions
- The "without crypto" alternative is vague ("the agent would just do it differently")
- Confuses blockchain storage with traditional database storage without explanation

---

## Checkpoint 3: Crypto+Agent Project Evaluation

**Domain:** Stage 3
**Type:** Structured writing
**Evaluator:** Self or peer

### Task

Given a crypto+agent project description (provided by evaluator or from a public source), write a 200-word evaluation that includes:

1. Which of the 5 crypto functions the project primarily uses
2. Which agent capability the project enables
3. One genuine strength and one unresolved problem
4. A 1-sentence verdict on whether the project represents genuine crypto utility or incidental integration

### Pass Criteria

- [ ] Correctly identifies the primary crypto function
- [ ] Correctly links it to an agent capability
- [ ] The strength is specific and derived from the project's approach
- [ ] The problem is specific (not just "early stage" or "needs more users")
- [ ] The verdict follows logically from the preceding analysis
- [ ] Entire evaluation is 150-250 words (not under 150 or over 300)

### Fail Indicators

- Misidentifies the primary crypto function (e.g., calls everything "payment")
- Lists multiple strengths or problems instead of selecting the most significant one
- The verdict contradicts the preceding analysis
- Evaluation is under 150 words (suggests insufficient depth) or over 300 words (suggests lack of precision)

---

## Checkpoint 4: The 5-Questions Framework

**Domain:** Stage 4
**Type:** Structured writing or presentation
**Evaluator:** Self or peer

### Task

Take a crypto+agent concept (provided or self-selected) and answer all 5 questions in writing:

1. What agent capability does crypto enable here?
2. What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?
3. What new risks does crypto introduce?
4. Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?
5. What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?

Then write a final verdict: **Genuine crypto utility** / **Mixed utility** / **Unnecessary crypto integration**

### Pass Criteria

- [ ] Question 1: Correctly identifies the specific crypto function and agent capability
- [ ] Question 2: Answer is specific about what breaks, not just "it would be slower" or "it wouldn't work"
- [ ] Question 3: Identifies real risks (security, regulatory, UX, liquidity, etc.) not generic ones
- [ ] Question 4: Justification is clear and based on questions 1-3
- [ ] Question 5: Addresses scaling implications for economics, infrastructure, or coordination
- [ ] Verdict matches the reasoning in questions 1-5
- [ ] All 5 questions answered with substantive content (not 1 sentence each)

### Fail Indicators

- Skips orж•·иЎҚ (perfunctorily answers) any of the 5 questions
- Question 2 answer is not specific (e.g., "it would be centralized")
- Question 4 answer does not connect to the preceding questions
- Verdict contradicts the body of the analysis
- Answers are so brief they suggest memorization rather than understanding

---

## Checkpoint Summary Table

| # | Checkpoint | Domain | Format | Pass Threshold |
|---|------------|--------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Agent Architecture + Crypto Functions | Stage 1 | Oral explanation | 5/5 criteria |
| 2 | Multi-function Crypto+Agent Scenario | Stage 2 | Written scenario | 5/5 criteria |
| 3 | Project Evaluation | Stage 3 | Structured writing | 6/6 criteria |
| 4 | 5-Questions Framework | Stage 4 | Structured writing | 7/7 criteria |

---

## How to Use These Checkpoints

### For Self-Assessment

1. Cover the pass criteria before starting
2. Complete the task without looking at the criteria
3. Check your work against the criteria honestly
4. If you fail any criterion, review the relevant stage before proceeding

### For Peer Assessment

1. Peer evaluator reads the pass criteria (but not to the person being evaluated)
2. The evaluated person completes the task
3. Peer evaluator checks each criterion and provides specific feedback on failures
4. Focus feedback on which specific criterion was not met, not global impressions

### Before Proceeding to Next Stage

Each checkpoint must be passed (all criteria met) before advancing to the next stage. If you cannot pass a checkpoint, revisit the learning content for that stage and try again after 24 hours.

1	# Learning Path: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	This learning path is structured in 4 stages, designed to take approximately 8-12 hours total for a general learner with basic knowledge of AI and cryptocurrency concepts.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Stage 1: Establish Boundaries and Core Concepts
10	
11	### Goal
12	Build a clear mental model of AI Agent architecture and cryptocurrency's core functions, without conflating the two.
13	
14	### Suggested Time
15	2-3 hours
16	
17	### Learning Content
18	
19	1. **AI Agent Architecture**
20	   - Understand the 4-component model: Perception, Reasoning, Action, Environment
21	   - Recognize that Agents are systems that observe, decide, and act autonomously
22	   - Key insight: Agents are not just "AI that does things" вҖ” they have structured inputs and outputs
23	
24	2. **Cryptocurrency Core Functions**
25	   - Review the 5 core functions relevant to agents: on-chain assets, wallets, payment networks, smart contracts, staking
26	   - Understand each function's basic mechanism (what it does, not how it is implemented technically)
27	   - Key insight: Crypto is not just "digital money" вҖ” it is a composable infrastructure toolkit
28	
29	3. **The Gap Between Them**
30	   - Recognize that AI systems and blockchain systems were built independently
31	   - Agents cannot "natively understand" blockchain вҖ” they need interfaces
32	
33	### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
34	- Deep technical details of how LLM reasoning works
35	- Consensus mechanisms or blockchain protocol internals
36	- Specific token economics or yield farming strategies
37	
38	### Completion Criteria
39	- [ ] Can explain AI Agent's 4-component model in your own words
40	- [ ] Can list cryptocurrency's 5 core functions relevant to agents
41	- [ ] Can articulate why a "gap" exists between AI systems and blockchain systems
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Stage 2: Understand How Crypto Extends Agent Capabilities
46	
47	### Goal
48	Move beyond crypto-as-payment to understand the full range of roles crypto plays for agents.
49	
50	### Suggested Time
51	2-3 hours
52	
53	### Learning Content
54	
55	1. **Payment Layer**
56	   - How agents use crypto to pay for services (compute, data, API calls)
57	   - How agents receive compensation for their own services
58	   - Micropayments and programmable settlement
59	
60	2. **Incentive Layer**
61	   - Token-based reward systems that motivate agent behavior
62	   - Stake-and-reward mechanisms
63	   - Bonding curves and economicжёёжҲҸзҡ„ Agent жҝҖеҠұи®ҫи®Ў
64	
65	3. **Identity Layer**
66	   - Wallet-based identity: public/private keys as agent identifiers
67	   - On-chain reputation: history of actions as credibility
68	   - Verifiable credentials and proof mechanisms
69	
70	4. **Storage/Memory Layer**
71	   - On-chain state as persistent memory
72	   - IPFS and decentralized storage for agent data
73	   - The tradeoffs: cost, latency, decentralization
74	
75	5. **Coordination Layer**
76	   - Smart contracts as agent-to-agent agreements
77	   - Decentralized agent registries and directories
78	   - Multi-agent workflows without centralized orchestrators
79	
80	### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
81	- How to implement specific smart contracts
82	- Zero-knowledge proof systems
83	- Detailed DeFi protocol mechanics
84	
85	### Completion Criteria
86	- [ ] Can describe at least 3 non-payment roles crypto plays for agents
87	- [ ] Can explain the basic tradeoff of on-chain vs off-chain storage for agent memory
88	- [ ] Can give one example of how crypto coordinates multiple agents
89	
90	---
91	
92	## Stage 3: Examine Real Projects and Applications
93	
94	### Goal
95	Connect abstract concepts to real-world crypto+agent projects, understanding what problems each solves.
96	
97	### Suggested Time
98	2-3 hours
99	
100	### Learning Content
101	
102	1. **Autonomous Economic Agents (AEA)**
103	   - What they are: agents that earn, spend, invest independently
104	   - How they use crypto: payment receipts, expense payment, stake management
105	   - Real project examples and their approaches
106	
107	2. **Agent Infrastructure Projects**
108	   - Projects that provide agents with crypto-native tools (wallets, payment rails, identity)
109	   - Frameworks that simplify crypto+agent integration
110	   - Data availability and compute networks for agents
111	
112	3. **DeFi Agent Strategies**
113	   - Automated trading agents that operate on-chain
114	   - Yield optimization agents
115	   - Risk management agents
116	
117	4. **On-chain Reasoning Agents**
118	   - Agents that analyze blockchain data
119	   - MEV capture agents
120	   - Governance participation agents
121	
122	### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
123	- Specific trading strategy formulas
124	- How to audit smart contract security
125	- How to build a production agent system
126	
127	### Completion Criteria
128	- [ ] Can categorize at least 3 real projects by their primary crypto+agent function
129	- [ ] Can identify which crypto capability (payment/incentive/identity/storage/coordination) each project primarily uses
130	- [ ] Can articulate one limitation or unsolved problem in current crypto+agent projects
131	
132	---
133	
134	## Stage 4: Form Your Systematic Framework
135	
136	### Goal
137	Synthesize learning into a personal cognitive framework for evaluating crypto's role in any agent application.
138	
139	### Suggested Time
140	1-2 hours
141	
142	### Learning Content
143	
144	1. **The Crypto+Agent Evaluation Matrix**
145	   - For any agent application, ask: Does it need crypto? Which crypto functions? What are the tradeoffs?
146	   - Distinguish genuine crypto utility from unnecessary crypto integration
147	
148	2. **Opportunity and Risk Assessment**
149	   - Key opportunities: programmable value, trustless coordination, decentralized identity, economic sovereignty
150	   - Key risks: technical immaturity, security vulnerabilities, regulatory uncertainty, user experience gaps
151	
152	3. **The 5 Questions Framework**
153	   For any crypto+agent application, answer:
154	   1. What agent capability does crypto enable here?
155	   2. What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?
156	   3. What new risks does crypto introduce?
157	   4. Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?
158	   5. What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?
159	
160	### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
161	- How to predict market outcomes
162	- Legal or compliance advice for specific jurisdictions
163	- How to build production-grade systems
164	
165	### Completion Criteria
166	- [ ] Can apply the 5 Questions Framework to a new crypto+agent concept
167	- [ ] Can distinguish genuine crypto utility from hype in a crypto+agent project
168	- [ ] Can articulate both the top opportunity and top risk for the crypto+agent intersection
169	
170	---
171	
172	## Path Summary Table
173	
174	| Stage | Focus | Time | Key Output |
175	|-------|-------|------|------------|
176	| 1 | Core concepts and boundaries | 2-3h | Mental models of Agent architecture and crypto functions |
177	| 2 | Crypto as capability extension | 2-3h | Understanding of 5 crypto roles for agents |
178	| 3 | Real projects examination | 2-3h | Categorization of existing projects |
179	| 4 | Systematic framework formation | 1-2h | Personal 5-questions evaluation tool |
180

1	# Exercises: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era
2	
3	These exercises are designed to be completed after each stage of the learning path. Each exercise is actionable and produces a concrete output.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Exercise 1: Decompose an AI Agent
8	
9	**Stage:** 1
10	**Time:** 30-45 minutes
11	**Format:** Written analysis
12	
13	### Instructions
14	
15	Select one of the following agent types (or choose your own):
16	- A news summarization agent
17	- A code review agent
18	- A personal finance agent
19	- A research assistant agent
20	
21	For your chosen agent, complete the following decomposition:
22	
23	1. **Perception component**: What inputs does this agent receive? List at least 3.
24	2. **Reasoning component**: What decisions does this agent make? List at least 3.
25	3. **Action component**: What outputs or actions does this agent produce? List at least 3.
26	4. **Environment component**: What external systems does this agent interact with?
27	
28	### Expected Output
29	
30	A structured document (300-500 words) that:
31	- Identifies all 4 components with specific examples
32	- Notes where blockchain/crypto systems could plausibly fit into the agent's architecture
33	- States one way crypto could NOT help this agent (misapplication check)
34	
35	### Verification Check
36	- Does your decomposition identify at least 3 inputs, 3 decisions, and 3 outputs?
37	- Did you identify at least 1 plausible crypto application AND 1 implausible crypto application?
38	
39	---
40	
41	## Exercise 2: Map Crypto Functions to Agent Needs
42	
43	**Stage:** 2
44	**Time:** 45-60 minutes
45	**Format:** Comparative table
46	
47	### Instructions
48	
49	Create a comparison table with the following structure:
50	
51	| Crypto Function | Agent Need It Addresses | Without Crypto, Agent Would... | Risk/Tradeoff Introduced |
52	|----------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
53	
54	Fill in at least 5 rows, covering different crypto functions from:
55	- Payment/settlement
56	- Incentive/stake
57	- Identity/authentication
58	- Storage/memory
59	- Coordination
60	
61	### Expected Output
62	
63	A table with 5+ rows, each containing:
64	- A crypto function correctly matched to an agent need
65	- A description of how the agent would function without crypto
66	- An honest risk or tradeoff introduced by using crypto
67	
68	### Verification Check
69	- Did you cover at least 4 of the 5 crypto functions?
70	- Does each row include a genuine risk/tradeoff (not just "it costs gas fees")?
71	- Is the "Without Crypto" column specific to an agent context?
72	
73	---
74	
75	## Exercise 3: Analyze a Real Crypto+Agent Project
76	
77	**Stage:** 3
78	**Time:** 60-90 minutes
79	**Format:** Project analysis brief
80	
81	### Instructions
82	
83	Research one of the following project categories (or find a specific project on your own):
84	- **Category A**: Agent frameworks with crypto integration (e.g., ixo, Autonolas, or similar)
85	- **Category B**: DeFi agent strategies (e.g., agents that automate trading or yield strategies)
86	- **Category C**: Decentralized AI infrastructure (e.g., compute or data availability networks)
87	
88	Write a 1-page analysis covering:
89	
90	1. **Project name and category** (2-3 sentences)
91	2. **What the project does** (succinct description)
92	3. **Which crypto functions does it use?** (list from: payment, incentive, identity, storage, coordination)
93	4. **Which Agent capabilities does it enable?**
94	5. **One genuine strength** of the approach
95	6. **One unresolved problem or limitation**
96	
97	### Expected Output
98	
99	A structured brief (400-600 words) with all 6 sections clearly labeled.
100	
101	### Verification Check
102	- Did you identify the project's primary crypto function?
103	- Did you identify the agent capability being enabled?
104	- Does your analysis include both a strength AND a limitation?
105	- Is the limitation specific (not just "early stage")?
106	
107	---
108	
109	## Exercise 4: Apply the 5-Questions Framework
110	
111	**Stage:** 4
112	**Time:** 45-60 minutes
113	**Format:** Structured evaluation
114	
115	### Instructions
116	
117	Select a hypothetical or real crypto+agent application that has NOT been covered in the learning path. For example:
118	- A social media agent that earns tokens for quality content moderation
119	- A healthcare agent that stores patient data on-chain
120	- A creative agent that registers AI-generated art as NFTs and manages licensing royalties
121	
122	Apply the 5-Questions Framework:
123	
124	1. **What agent capability does crypto enable here?**
125	2. **What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?**
126	3. **What new risks does crypto introduce?**
127	4. **Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?**
128	5. **What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?**
129	
130	### Expected Output
131	
132	A structured document (400-500 words) that:
133	- Names and describes the application being evaluated
134	- Answers each of the 5 questions with specific reasoning
135	- Includes a final verdict: "Genuine crypto utility" / "Mixed utility" / "Unnecessary crypto integration"
136	
137	### Verification Check
138	- Did you answer all 5 questions?
139	- Is the verdict supported by the answers to the preceding questions?
140	- Did you consider scaling implications (question 5)?
141	
142	---
143	
144	## Exercise 5: Identify Misconceptions in Crypto+Agent Claims
145	
146	**Stage:** 4 (capstone)
147	**Time:** 30-45 minutes
148	**Format:** Correction exercise
149	
150	### Instructions
151	
152	Review the following claims (or find 3 claims from crypto+agent articles, social posts, or project documentation online). For each claim:
153	
154	1. State whether the claim is **correct**, **partially correct**, or **misleading**
155	2. If misleading or partially correct, explain why in 2-3 sentences
156	3. Suggest a more accurate framing
157	
158	### Claims to Analyze
159	
160	1. "AI Agents will make cryptocurrency obsolete by replacing smart contracts with AI reasoning."
161	2. "Crypto wallets are just like bank accounts for agents."
162	3. "Any AI Agent can automatically interact with any blockchain without additional infrastructure."
163	4. "The main value of crypto for agents is faster payments."
164	5. "Once AI Agents are mainstream, crypto will be unnecessary because AI can handle all coordination."
165	
166	### Expected Output
167	
168	A table or list with 5 claims, each labeled and corrected.
169	
170	### Verification Check
171	- Did you correctly identify at least 3 claims as misleading or partially correct?
172	- Are your corrections specific and grounded in concepts from the learning path?
173	- Do corrections reference specific crypto functions or agent capabilities?
174	
175	---
176	
177	## Exercise Summary
178	
179	| # | Exercise | Stage | Time | Output |
180	|---|----------|-------|------|--------|
181	| 1 | Decompose an AI Agent | 1 | 30-45min | Written decomposition |
182	| 2 | Map Crypto to Agent Needs | 2 | 45-60min | Comparison table |
183	| 3 | Analyze a Real Project | 3 | 60-90min | Project brief |
184	| 4 | Apply 5-Questions Framework | 4 | 45-60min | Evaluation document |
185	| 5 | Identify Misconceptions | 4 | 30-45min | Correction table |
186

1	# Checkpoints: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era
2	
3	Checkpoints are verifiable milestones that confirm genuine understanding, not just familiarity with terms. Each checkpoint requires active demonstration, not passive recognition.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Checkpoint 1: AI Agent Architecture and Crypto Core Functions
8	
9	**Domain:** Stage 1
10	**Type:** Oral or written explanation
11	**Evaluator:** Self or peer
12	
13	### Task
14	
15	Explain the following to a non-technical person in 3-5 minutes:
16	
17	1. What an AI Agent is (using the 4-component model: Perception, Reasoning, Action, Environment)
18	2. What cryptocurrency's 5 core functions are (on-chain assets, wallets, payment network, smart contracts, staking)
19	3. Why these two systems were historically separate and what the "integration gap" is
20	
21	### Pass Criteria
22	
23	- [ ] Explains all 4 agent components with at least one concrete example for each
24	- [ ] Lists all 5 crypto functions correctly
25	- [ ] Articulates the integration gap without using technical jargon
26	- [ ] Uses no more than 2 minutes on agent architecture and 2 minutes on crypto functions
27	- [ ] Answers one follow-up question about either component correctly
28	
29	### Fail Indicators
30	
31	- Skips or conflates agent components (e.g., "the AI does everything")
32	- Lists fewer than 5 crypto functions or includes non-crypto functions
33	- Cannot explain why agents cannot natively "understand" blockchain
34	- Uses jargon without explanation when speaking to non-technical audience
35	
36	---
37	
38	## Checkpoint 2: Crypto Roles for Agents
39	
40	**Domain:** Stage 2
41	**Type:** Written or diagram-based demonstration
42	**Evaluator:** Self-check against criteria
43	
44	### Task
45	
46	Complete the following task without referring to source materials:
47	
48	Draw or describe a scenario where a single AI Agent uses at least 3 different crypto functions simultaneously. For each function used, specify:
49	- Which crypto function is being used
50	- What problem it solves for the agent
51	- What the agent would do if that crypto function were unavailable
52	
53	### Pass Criteria
54	
55	- [ ] Describes a scenario with at least 3 distinct crypto functions
56	- [ ] Each function is correctly identified and matched to a genuine agent need
57	- [ ] For each function, the alternative (without crypto) is specific, not generic
58	- [ ] The scenario is coherent and internally consistent
59	- [ ] Storage/memory and coordination roles are not confused with payment
60	
61	### Fail Indicators
62	
63	- Describes a scenario that only uses crypto as payment
64	- Uses fewer than 3 crypto functions
65	- The "without crypto" alternative is vague ("the agent would just do it differently")
66	- Confuses blockchain storage with traditional database storage without explanation
67	
68	---
69	
70	## Checkpoint 3: Crypto+Agent Project Evaluation
71	
72	**Domain:** Stage 3
73	**Type:** Structured writing
74	**Evaluator:** Self or peer
75	
76	### Task
77	
78	Given a crypto+agent project description (provided by evaluator or from a public source), write a 200-word evaluation that includes:
79	
80	1. Which of the 5 crypto functions the project primarily uses
81	2. Which agent capability the project enables
82	3. One genuine strength and one unresolved problem
83	4. A 1-sentence verdict on whether the project represents genuine crypto utility or incidental integration
84	
85	### Pass Criteria
86	
87	- [ ] Correctly identifies the primary crypto function
88	- [ ] Correctly links it to an agent capability
89	- [ ] The strength is specific and derived from the project's approach
90	- [ ] The problem is specific (not just "early stage" or "needs more users")
91	- [ ] The verdict follows logically from the preceding analysis
92	- [ ] Entire evaluation is 150-250 words (not under 150 or over 300)
93	
94	### Fail Indicators
95	
96	- Misidentifies the primary crypto function (e.g., calls everything "payment")
97	- Lists multiple strengths or problems instead of selecting the most significant one
98	- The verdict contradicts the preceding analysis
99	- Evaluation is under 150 words (suggests insufficient depth) or over 300 words (suggests lack of precision)
100	
101	---
102	
103	## Checkpoint 4: The 5-Questions Framework
104	
105	**Domain:** Stage 4
106	**Type:** Structured writing or presentation
107	**Evaluator:** Self or peer
108	
109	### Task
110	
111	Take a crypto+agent concept (provided or self-selected) and answer all 5 questions in writing:
112	
113	1. What agent capability does crypto enable here?
114	2. What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?
115	3. What new risks does crypto introduce?
116	4. Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?
117	5. What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?
118	
119	Then write a final verdict: **Genuine crypto utility** / **Mixed utility** / **Unnecessary crypto integration**
120	
121	### Pass Criteria
122	
123	- [ ] Question 1: Correctly identifies the specific crypto function and agent capability
124	- [ ] Question 2: Answer is specific about what breaks, not just "it would be slower" or "it wouldn't work"
125	- [ ] Question 3: Identifies real risks (security, regulatory, UX, liquidity, etc.) not generic ones
126	- [ ] Question 4: Justification is clear and based on questions 1-3
127	- [ ] Question 5: Addresses scaling implications for economics, infrastructure, or coordination
128	- [ ] Verdict matches the reasoning in questions 1-5
129	- [ ] All 5 questions answered with substantive content (not 1 sentence each)
130	
131	### Fail Indicators
132	
133	- Skips orж•·иЎҚ (perfunctorily answers) any of the 5 questions
134	- Question 2 answer is not specific (e.g., "it would be centralized")
135	- Question 4 answer does not connect to the preceding questions
136	- Verdict contradicts the body of the analysis
137	- Answers are so brief they suggest memorization rather than understanding
138	
139	---
140	
141	## Checkpoint Summary Table
142	
143	| # | Checkpoint | Domain | Format | Pass Threshold |
144	|---|------------|--------|--------|----------------|
145	| 1 | Agent Architecture + Crypto Functions | Stage 1 | Oral explanation | 5/5 criteria |
146	| 2 | Multi-function Crypto+Agent Scenario | Stage 2 | Written scenario | 5/5 criteria |
147	| 3 | Project Evaluation | Stage 3 | Structured writing | 6/6 criteria |
148	| 4 | 5-Questions Framework | Stage 4 | Structured writing | 7/7 criteria |
149	
150	---
151	
152	## How to Use These Checkpoints
153	
154	### For Self-Assessment
155	
156	1. Cover the pass criteria before starting
157	2. Complete the task without looking at the criteria
158	3. Check your work against the criteria honestly
159	4. If you fail any criterion, review the relevant stage before proceeding
160	
161	### For Peer Assessment
162	
163	1. Peer evaluator reads the pass criteria (but not to the person being evaluated)
164	2. The evaluated person completes the task
165	3. Peer evaluator checks each criterion and provides specific feedback on failures
166	4. Focus feedback on which specific criterion was not met, not global impressions
167	
168	### Before Proceeding to Next Stage
169	
170	Each checkpoint must be passed (all criteria met) before advancing to the next stage. If you cannot pass a checkpoint, revisit the learning content for that stage and try again after 24 hours.
171

# Task02 Evaluation Report

## з¬¬ 1 ж¬ЎиҜ„дј°

### еҲӨе®ҡпјҡPASS

| з»ҙеәҰ | еҲҶж•° | йҳҲеҖј | з»“и®ә |

| Clarity | 5 | 4 | PASS |
| Actionability | 5 | 4 | PASS |
| User Context Fit | 5 | 4 | PASS |
| Transferability | 5 | 4 | PASS |

## иҜ„дј°иҜҙжҳҺ

**Question Quality**пјҡlearning-path.md еҮҶзЎ®йҮҚиҝ°дәҶеҺҹе§Ӣй—®йўҳпјҲеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јзҡ„дҪңз”ЁпјүпјҢжҜҸдёӘйҳ¶ж®өйғҪжңүжҳҺзЎ®зҡ„зӣ®ж ҮпјҲGoalпјүе’Ңе®ҢжҲҗж ҮеҮҶпјҲCompletion CriteriaпјүгҖӮ4дёӘйҳ¶ж®өеҜ№еә”learning-contractдёӯ4дёӘеӯҰд№ зӣ®ж ҮпјҡзҗҶи§Јж ёеҝғжңәеҲ¶пјҲStage 1пјүвҶ’ еҲҶжһҗеҹәзЎҖи®ҫж–ҪдҪңз”ЁпјҲStage 2пјүвҶ’ еҲӨж–ӯдёҚеҗҢеңәжҷҜи§’иүІпјҲStage 3пјүвҶ’ еҪўжҲҗзі»з»ҹи®ӨзҹҘжЎҶжһ¶пјҲStage 4пјүгҖӮиҫ№з•Ңжё…жҷ°пјҢ"What You Will NOT Learn"йғЁеҲҶдёҺcontractдёӯ"жҡӮдёҚиҰҶзӣ–"дёҖиҮҙгҖӮ

**Coverage**пјҡе®Ңж•ҙиҰҶзӣ–learning-contractдёӯжүҖжңү5дёӘеҝ…йЎ»иҰҶзӣ–зҡ„ж ёеҝғжҰӮеҝөпјҡ
1. AI Agentеҹәжң¬жһ¶жһ„пјҲStage 1пјҢ4-component modelпјү
2. еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеҹәзЎҖпјҲStage 1пјҢ5 core functionsпјү
3. AgentиғҪеҠӣжү©еұ•пјҲStage 2пјҢ5 layers: payment/incentive/identity/storage/coordinationпјү
4. иҮӘдё»з»ҸжөҺд»ЈзҗҶпјҲStage 3пјҢAEAжҰӮеҝөпјү
5. зҺ°жңүcrypto+agentйЎ№зӣ®з”ҹжҖҒпјҲStage 3пјҢ4 categoriesпјү
жүҖжңүдё»е№ІгҖҒеҲҶж”ҜгҖҒиҫ№зјҳиҜқйўҳеқҮжңүеҢәеҲҶгҖӮ

**Clarity**пјҡStage 1-4з»“жһ„жё…жҷ°пјҢжҜҸйҳ¶ж®өеҢ…еҗ«GoalгҖҒSuggested TimeгҖҒLearning ContentгҖҒCompletion CriteriaгҖӮиЎЁж јз”ЁдәҺеҜ№жҜ”crypto functionsгҖӮStage 4зҡ„"5 Questions Framework"зӣҙжҺҘз»ҷеҮәе…·дҪ“й—®йўҳдҫӣеӯҰд№ иҖ…дҪҝз”ЁгҖӮexercises.mdжҜҸйЎ№еҢ…еҗ«TimeгҖҒFormatгҖҒInstructionsгҖҒExpected OutputгҖҒVerification Checkдә”йЎ№гҖӮ"What You Will NOT Learn"дҪҝиҫ№з•ҢдёҖзӣ®дәҶз„¶гҖӮ

**Actionability**пјҡеӯҰд№ и·Ҝеҫ„жңүйЎәеәҸпјҲStage 1вҶ’2вҶ’3вҶ’4пјүпјҢжҜҸйҳ¶ж®өжңүзӣ®ж ҮпјҲGoalпјүе’Ңе®ҢжҲҗж ҮеҮҶпјҲCompletion Criteria checklistпјүгҖӮ5дёӘз»ғд№ еқҮе…·дҪ“еҸҜжү§иЎҢпјҡExercise 1иҰҒжұӮеҲҶи§Јagentиҫ“еҮә300-500еӯ—ж–ҮжЎЈпјҢExercise 2иҰҒжұӮеҲ¶дҪң5иЎҢеҜ№жҜ”иЎЁпјҢExercise 3иҰҒжұӮеҶҷ400-600еӯ—йЎ№зӣ®з®ҖжҠҘпјҢExercise 4иҰҒжұӮз”Ё5й—®йўҳжЎҶжһ¶иҜ„дј°е№¶з»ҷеҮәеҲӨеҶіпјҢExercise 5иҰҒжұӮеҲҶжһҗ5дёӘclaimе№¶зә жӯЈгҖӮcheckpoints.mdжҜҸйЎ№checkpointжңүжҳҺзЎ®зҡ„Pass CriteriaпјҲ5/5/6/7йЎ№жү“еӢҫж ҮеҮҶпјүе’ҢFail IndicatorsгҖӮжңҖе°ҸдёӢдёҖжӯҘпјҡStage 1зҡ„"AI Agent Architecture"д»Һ4-component modelејҖе§ӢпјҢеҪ“еӨ©еҸҜиҜ»гҖӮ

**User Context Fit**пјҡlearning-contractжҳҺзЎ®еҶҷжҳҺ"жңӘжҢҮе®ҡпјӣжҢүйҖҡз”ЁеӯҰд№ иҖ…еӨ„зҗҶ"гҖӮдә§зү©е…ЁзЁӢдҪҝз”ЁйҖҡз”ЁжҰӮеҝөпјҢжңӘеј•е…Ҙд»»дҪ•дёӘдәә/иЎҢдёҡ/иҒҢдёҡ/дә§е“ҒиғҢжҷҜгҖӮжүҖжңүcryptoеҮҪж•°е’ҢagentиғҪеҠӣйғҪжҳҜйҖҡз”ЁеҹәзЎҖи®ҫж–ҪжҰӮеҝөпјҢж— иЎҢдёҡзү№е®ҡеңәжҷҜз»‘е®ҡгҖӮ

**Transferability**пјҡStage 4зҡ„"5 Questions Framework"еҸҜиҝҒз§»дёәиҜ„дј°д»»дҪ•crypto+agentеә”з”Ёзҡ„е…·дҪ“е·Ҙе…·гҖӮExercise 5дё“й—Ёи®ӯз»ғиҜҶеҲ«еёёи§ҒиҜҜи§ЈпјҲmisconceptionsпјүпјҢиҝҷжҳҜиҝҒз§»и§ЈйҮҠиғҪеҠӣзҡ„е…ій”®жҠҖиғҪгҖӮExercise 4иҰҒжұӮеҜ№е…Ёж–°еә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜеә”з”ЁжЎҶжһ¶пјҢејәеҲ¶з»ғд№ и·ЁеңәжҷҜиҝҒз§»гҖӮжүҖжңүз»ғд№ дә§еҮәжҳҺзЎ®пјҡж–ҮжЎЈгҖҒиЎЁж јгҖҒз®ҖжҠҘгҖҒиҜ„д»·гҖҒзә жӯЈиЎЁвҖ”вҖ”еҸҜдҪңдёәеӯҰд№ иҖ…зҡ„е…·дҪ“дҪңе“ҒгҖӮ

## дә§зү©дёҖиҮҙжҖ§жЈҖжҹҘ

- learning-path.md Stage 3зҡ„4дёӘйЎ№зӣ®зұ»еҲ«пјҲAgent frameworks, DeFi strategies, AI infrastructure, Autonomous platformsпјүдёҺdomain-map.md Section 2.2иЎЁж јдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ
- learning-path.md Stage 4зҡ„"5 Questions Framework"дёҺdomain-map.md Section 6зҡ„5дёӘKey QuestionsзӣҙжҺҘеҜ№еә”гҖӮ
- checkpoints.mdзҡ„4дёӘcheckpointиҰҶзӣ–Stage 1-4пјҢжҜҸдёӘдёҺеҜ№еә”йҳ¶ж®өзҡ„Completion CriteriaдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ
- exercises.mdзҡ„Exercise 3йЎ№зӣ®зұ»еҲ«дёҺlearning-path.md Stage 3йЎ№зӣ®еҲ—иЎЁдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ

## Task 2 дёҺ Task 1 иЎ”жҺҘжЈҖжҹҘ

- Stage 1зҡ„"AI Agent Architecture"4-component modelдёҺdomain-map.md Section 1.1иЎЁж јдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ

1	# Question Brief
2	
3	## Original Question
4	
5	еҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲдҪңз”Ё
6	
7	## Learning Objectives
8	
9	After completing this learning path, you will be able to:
10	
11	- Understand the core mechanisms of interaction between cryptocurrency and AI Agents
12	- Analyze the role of cryptocurrency as Agent infrastructure
13	- Judge the role of cryptocurrency in different Agent application scenarios
14	- Form a systematic cognitive framework for the "crypto + agent" intersection
15	
16	## Boundaries and Non-Objectives
17	
18	### What You Will Learn
19	
20	- AI Agent basic architecture (perception, reasoning, action, environment interaction)
21	- Cryptocurrency core functions (on-chain assets, wallets, payment networks, smart contracts)
22	- How crypto extends Agent capabilities (as payment, incentive, identity, storage layer)
23	- Autonomous Economic Agent (AEA) concept
24	- Overview of existing crypto+agent project ecosystem
25	
26	### What You Will NOT Learn
27	
28	- Specific cryptocurrency price trends or investment advice
29	- Detailed on-chain protocol implementation details
30	- Non-crypto AI Agent development technologies (RAG, Fine-tuning)
31	- Compliance and legal analysis
32	
33	## Prerequisites
34	
35	- Basic understanding of what AI is (broad concept, not deep technical knowledge)
36	- Basic understanding of what cryptocurrency is (broad concept)
37	- No prior knowledge of AI Agent architecture required
38	- No prior knowledge of crypto+agent intersection required
39	
40	## Common Misconceptions
41	
42	1. **"Crypto is just a payment method for AI"** вҖ” In the Agent era, crypto serves multiple roles: payment, incentive mechanism, identity layer, stake mechanism, and long-term memory storage.
43	
44	2. **"AI Agents automatically understand blockchain"** вҖ” There is a significant technical gap between traditional AI systems and blockchain interaction. Agents need specific tools and interfaces to operate on-chain.
45	
46	3. **"Crypto+agent is only about financial agents"** вҖ” Beyond trading bots, crypto+agent covers identity, coordination, governance, and infrastructure roles.
47	
48	4. **"Agents will replace all crypto applications"** вҖ” Agents are a new interface layer; crypto protocols remain the underlying infrastructure with their own logic and tradeoffs.
49	
50	5. **"The technology is already mature and production-ready"** вҖ” The crypto+agent space is still early-stage; many projects are experimental and infrastructure is rapidly evolving.

1	# Domain Map: Crypto + Agent Intersection
2	
3	## Core Concept Relationship
4	
5	```
6	AI Agent (perceives, reasons, acts)
7	       в”Ӯ
8	       в”Ӯ needs capabilities
9	       в–ј
10	в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
11	в”Ӯ  Crypto Layer (as infrastructure)        в”Ӯ
12	в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
13	в”Ӯ вҖў Payment/settlement                    в”Ӯ
14	в”Ӯ вҖў Incentive/stake                       в”Ӯ
15	в”Ӯ вҖў Identity/authentication                в”Ӯ
16	в”Ӯ вҖў Storage/memory                        в”Ӯ
17	в”Ӯ вҖў Coordination/computation               в”Ӯ
18	в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
19	```
20	
21	---
22	
23	## 1. Main Trunk Concepts
24	
25	### 1.1 AI Agent Architecture
26	
27	| Component | Role | Crypto Relevance |
28	|---|---|---|
29	| Perception | Receives external input (APIs, user requests, environment data) | Can receive on-chain events as input |
30	| Reasoning | Processes information, makes decisions | May consider crypto incentives in decisions |
31	| Action | Executes tasks | Can trigger on-chain transactions |
32	| Environment | Interacts with external systems | Blockchain as environment |
33	
34	### 1.2 Cryptocurrency Core Functions
35	
36	| Function | Description | Agent Application |
37	|---|---|---|
38	| On-chain Assets | Tokens, NFTs, digital property | Agent holds and manages assets |
39	| Wallets | Cryptographic key management | Agent identity and ownership |
40	| Payment Network | Value transfer settlement | Agent receives compensation, pays for services |
41	| Smart Contracts | Programmable logic execution | Agent runs autonomous code |
42	| Staking/Locking | Economic commitment | Agent stakes to secure operations, earn rewards |
43	
44	### 1.3 Agent Capability Extensions via Crypto
45	
46	- **Payment Layer**: Agents pay for services, receive compensation, settle transactions
47	- **Incentive Layer**: Token-based reward systems motivate agent behavior
48	- **Identity Layer**: Wallet-based identity, reputation tied to on-chain history
49	- **Storage Layer**: On-chain state, IPFS, and decentralized storage for agent memory
50	- **Coordination Layer**: Agents coordinate via smart contracts without centralized intermediaries
51	
52	---
53	
54	## 2. Branch Topics
55	
56	### 2.1 Application Scenarios
57	
58	- **Autonomous Economic Agents (AEA)**: Agents that earn, spend, and invest independently
59	- **Decentralized AI Services**: Agent networks powered by crypto incentives
60	- **On-chain Reasoning**: Agents that analyze and act on blockchain data
61	- **Token-gated Access**: Agents unlock services via crypto stakes or holdings
62	
63	### 2.2 Existing Project Categories
64	
65	| Category | Example Function |
66	|---|---|
67	| Agent Frameworks with Crypto | Protocol-level agent toolkits |
68	| DeFi Agent Strategies | Automated trading, yield optimization |
69	| AI + Blockchain Infrastructure | Compute networks, data availability |
70	| Autonomous Agent Platforms | Agent-to-agent coordination protocols |
71	
72	---
73	
74	## 3. Edge Topics (Not Covered in This Learning Path)
75	
76	- Deep technical implementation of specific consensus mechanisms
77	- Cryptocurrency trading strategies and market analysis
78	- Legal and regulatory compliance frameworks
79	- Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning techniques
80	- ZK-proof technical details
81	
82	---
83	
84	## 4. Prerequisites Map
85	
86	```
87	Level 1: Basic Awareness
88	в”ңв”Җв”Җ What is AI (broad concept)
89	в””в”Җв”Җ What is Cryptocurrency (broad concept)
90	
91	Level 2: Foundational Understanding
92	в”ңв”Җв”Җ How AI Agents work (basic architecture)
93	в””в”Җв”Җ How blockchain/crypto works (basic functions)
94	
95	Level 3: Intersection Understanding
96	в”ңв”Җв”Җ Why crypto is relevant to agents
97	в””в”Җв”Җ What problems does crypto solve for agents
98	```
99	
100	---
101	
102	## 5. Common Misconceptions Map
103	
104	| Misconception | Reality |
105	|---|---|
106	| "Crypto is just digital payment" | Crypto provides payment, identity, storage, coordination |
107	| "Agents can read blockchain naturally" | Agents need specific tools/interfaces to interact with chains |
108	| "Crypto+agent is only trading bots" | Covers identity, coordination, governance, infrastructure |
109	| "Agents replace crypto protocols" | Agents are interface layer; crypto remains underlying infrastructure |
110	| "Technology is production-ready" | Space is early-stage, experimental, rapidly evolving |
111	
112	---
113	
114	## 6. Key Questions Structure
115	
116	This domain can be understood by answering five core questions:
117	
118	1. **What are AI Agents?** вҖ” Core architecture and capabilities
119	2. **What is cryptocurrency's role?** вҖ” Beyond payments to infrastructure functions
120	3. **What new demands does Agent era place on crypto?** вҖ” New use cases and requirements
121	4. **What existing projects exist?** вҖ” Real examples and solutions
122	5. **What are opportunities and risks?** вҖ” Strategic outlook for the intersection
