1	# Learning Path: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	This learning path is structured in 4 stages, designed to take approximately 8-12 hours total for a general learner with basic knowledge of AI and cryptocurrency concepts.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Stage 1: Establish Boundaries and Core Concepts
10	
11	### Goal
12	Build a clear mental model of AI Agent architecture and cryptocurrency's core functions, without conflating the two.
13	
14	### Suggested Time
15	2-3 hours
16	
17	### Learning Content
18	
19	1. **AI Agent Architecture**
20	   - Understand the 4-component model: Perception, Reasoning, Action, Environment
21	   - Recognize that Agents are systems that observe, decide, and act autonomously
22	   - Key insight: Agents are not just "AI that does things" вҖ” they have structured inputs and outputs
23	
24	2. **Cryptocurrency Core Functions**
25	   - Review the 5 core functions relevant to agents: on-chain assets, wallets, payment networks, smart contracts, staking
26	   - Understand each function's basic mechanism (what it does, not how it is implemented technically)
27	   - Key insight: Crypto is not just "digital money" вҖ” it is a composable infrastructure toolkit
28	
29	3. **The Gap Between Them**
30	   - Recognize that AI systems and blockchain systems were built independently
31	   - Agents cannot "natively understand" blockchain вҖ” they need interfaces
32	
33	### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
34	- Deep technical details of how LLM reasoning works
35	- Consensus mechanisms or blockchain protocol internals
36	- Specific token economics or yield farming strategies
37	
38	### Completion Criteria
39	- [ ] Can explain AI Agent's 4-component model in your own words
40	- [ ] Can list cryptocurrency's 5 core functions relevant to agents
41	- [ ] Can articulate why a "gap" exists between AI systems and blockchain systems
42	
43	---
44	
45	## Stage 2: Understand How Crypto Extends Agent Capabilities
46	
47	### Goal
48	Move beyond crypto-as-payment to understand the full range of roles crypto plays for agents.
49	
50	### Suggested Time
51	2-3 hours
52	
53	### Learning Content
54	
55	1. **Payment Layer**
56	   - How agents use crypto to pay for services (compute, data, API calls)
57	   - How agents receive compensation for their own services
58	   - Micropayments and programmable settlement
59	
60	2. **Incentive Layer**
61	   - Token-based reward systems that motivate agent behavior
62	   - Stake-and-reward mechanisms
63	   - Bonding curves and economicжёёжҲҸзҡ„ Agent жҝҖеҠұи®ҫи®Ў
64	
65	3. **Identity Layer**
66	   - Wallet-based identity: public/private keys as agent identifiers
67	   - On-chain reputation: history of actions as credibility
68	   - Verifiable credentials and proof mechanisms
69	
70	4. **Storage/Memory Layer**
71	   - On-chain state as persistent memory
72	   - IPFS and decentralized storage for agent data
73	   - The tradeoffs: cost, latency, decentralization
74	
75	5. **Coordination Layer**
76	   - Smart contracts as agent-to-agent agreements
77	   - Decentralized agent registries and directories
78	   - Multi-agent workflows without centralized orchestrators
79	
80	### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
81	- How to implement specific smart contracts
82	- Zero-knowledge proof systems
83	- Detailed DeFi protocol mechanics
84	
85	### Completion Criteria
86	- [ ] Can describe at least 3 non-payment roles crypto plays for agents
87	- [ ] Can explain the basic tradeoff of on-chain vs off-chain storage for agent memory
88	- [ ] Can give one example of how crypto coordinates multiple agents
89	
90	---
91	
92	## Stage 3: Examine Real Projects and Applications
93	
94	### Goal
95	Connect abstract concepts to real-world crypto+agent projects, understanding what problems each solves.
96	
97	### Suggested Time
98	2-3 hours
99	
100	### Learning Content
101	
102	1. **Autonomous Economic Agents (AEA)**
103	   - What they are: agents that earn, spend, invest independently
104	   - How they use crypto: payment receipts, expense payment, stake management
105	   - Real project examples and their approaches
106	
107	2. **Agent Infrastructure Projects**
108	   - Projects that provide agents with crypto-native tools (wallets, payment rails, identity)
109	   - Frameworks that simplify crypto+agent integration
110	   - Data availability and compute networks for agents
111	
112	3. **DeFi Agent Strategies**
113	   - Automated trading agents that operate on-chain
114	   - Yield optimization agents
115	   - Risk management agents
116	
117	4. **On-chain Reasoning Agents**
118	   - Agents that analyze blockchain data
119	   - MEV capture agents
120	   - Governance participation agents
121	
122	### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
123	- Specific trading strategy formulas
124	- How to audit smart contract security
125	- How to build a production agent system
126	
127	### Completion Criteria
128	- [ ] Can categorize at least 3 real projects by their primary crypto+agent function
129	- [ ] Can identify which crypto capability (payment/incentive/identity/storage/coordination) each project primarily uses
130	- [ ] Can articulate one limitation or unsolved problem in current crypto+agent projects
131	
132	---
133	
134	## Stage 4: Form Your Systematic Framework
135	
136	### Goal
137	Synthesize learning into a personal cognitive framework for evaluating crypto's role in any agent application.
138	
139	### Suggested Time
140	1-2 hours
141	
142	### Learning Content
143	
144	1. **The Crypto+Agent Evaluation Matrix**
145	   - For any agent application, ask: Does it need crypto? Which crypto functions? What are the tradeoffs?
146	   - Distinguish genuine crypto utility from unnecessary crypto integration
147	
148	2. **Opportunity and Risk Assessment**
149	   - Key opportunities: programmable value, trustless coordination, decentralized identity, economic sovereignty
150	   - Key risks: technical immaturity, security vulnerabilities, regulatory uncertainty, user experience gaps
151	
152	3. **The 5 Questions Framework**
153	   For any crypto+agent application, answer:
154	   1. What agent capability does crypto enable here?
155	   2. What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?
156	   3. What new risks does crypto introduce?
157	   4. Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?
158	   5. What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?
159	
160	### What You Will NOT Learn in This Stage
161	- How to predict market outcomes
162	- Legal or compliance advice for specific jurisdictions
163	- How to build production-grade systems
164	
165	### Completion Criteria
166	- [ ] Can apply the 5 Questions Framework to a new crypto+agent concept
167	- [ ] Can distinguish genuine crypto utility from hype in a crypto+agent project
168	- [ ] Can articulate both the top opportunity and top risk for the crypto+agent intersection
169	
170	---
171	
172	## Path Summary Table
173	
174	| Stage | Focus | Time | Key Output |
175	|-------|-------|------|------------|
176	| 1 | Core concepts and boundaries | 2-3h | Mental models of Agent architecture and crypto functions |
177	| 2 | Crypto as capability extension | 2-3h | Understanding of 5 crypto roles for agents |
178	| 3 | Real projects examination | 2-3h | Categorization of existing projects |
179	| 4 | Systematic framework formation | 1-2h | Personal 5-questions evaluation tool |
180

1	# Exercises: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era
2	
3	These exercises are designed to be completed after each stage of the learning path. Each exercise is actionable and produces a concrete output.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Exercise 1: Decompose an AI Agent
8	
9	**Stage:** 1
10	**Time:** 30-45 minutes
11	**Format:** Written analysis
12	
13	### Instructions
14	
15	Select one of the following agent types (or choose your own):
16	- A news summarization agent
17	- A code review agent
18	- A personal finance agent
19	- A research assistant agent
20	
21	For your chosen agent, complete the following decomposition:
22	
23	1. **Perception component**: What inputs does this agent receive? List at least 3.
24	2. **Reasoning component**: What decisions does this agent make? List at least 3.
25	3. **Action component**: What outputs or actions does this agent produce? List at least 3.
26	4. **Environment component**: What external systems does this agent interact with?
27	
28	### Expected Output
29	
30	A structured document (300-500 words) that:
31	- Identifies all 4 components with specific examples
32	- Notes where blockchain/crypto systems could plausibly fit into the agent's architecture
33	- States one way crypto could NOT help this agent (misapplication check)
34	
35	### Verification Check
36	- Does your decomposition identify at least 3 inputs, 3 decisions, and 3 outputs?
37	- Did you identify at least 1 plausible crypto application AND 1 implausible crypto application?
38	
39	---
40	
41	## Exercise 2: Map Crypto Functions to Agent Needs
42	
43	**Stage:** 2
44	**Time:** 45-60 minutes
45	**Format:** Comparative table
46	
47	### Instructions
48	
49	Create a comparison table with the following structure:
50	
51	| Crypto Function | Agent Need It Addresses | Without Crypto, Agent Would... | Risk/Tradeoff Introduced |
52	|----------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
53	
54	Fill in at least 5 rows, covering different crypto functions from:
55	- Payment/settlement
56	- Incentive/stake
57	- Identity/authentication
58	- Storage/memory
59	- Coordination
60	
61	### Expected Output
62	
63	A table with 5+ rows, each containing:
64	- A crypto function correctly matched to an agent need
65	- A description of how the agent would function without crypto
66	- An honest risk or tradeoff introduced by using crypto
67	
68	### Verification Check
69	- Did you cover at least 4 of the 5 crypto functions?
70	- Does each row include a genuine risk/tradeoff (not just "it costs gas fees")?
71	- Is the "Without Crypto" column specific to an agent context?
72	
73	---
74	
75	## Exercise 3: Analyze a Real Crypto+Agent Project
76	
77	**Stage:** 3
78	**Time:** 60-90 minutes
79	**Format:** Project analysis brief
80	
81	### Instructions
82	
83	Research one of the following project categories (or find a specific project on your own):
84	- **Category A**: Agent frameworks with crypto integration (e.g., ixo, Autonolas, or similar)
85	- **Category B**: DeFi agent strategies (e.g., agents that automate trading or yield strategies)
86	- **Category C**: Decentralized AI infrastructure (e.g., compute or data availability networks)
87	
88	Write a 1-page analysis covering:
89	
90	1. **Project name and category** (2-3 sentences)
91	2. **What the project does** (succinct description)
92	3. **Which crypto functions does it use?** (list from: payment, incentive, identity, storage, coordination)
93	4. **Which Agent capabilities does it enable?**
94	5. **One genuine strength** of the approach
95	6. **One unresolved problem or limitation**
96	
97	### Expected Output
98	
99	A structured brief (400-600 words) with all 6 sections clearly labeled.
100	
101	### Verification Check
102	- Did you identify the project's primary crypto function?
103	- Did you identify the agent capability being enabled?
104	- Does your analysis include both a strength AND a limitation?
105	- Is the limitation specific (not just "early stage")?
106	
107	---
108	
109	## Exercise 4: Apply the 5-Questions Framework
110	
111	**Stage:** 4
112	**Time:** 45-60 minutes
113	**Format:** Structured evaluation
114	
115	### Instructions
116	
117	Select a hypothetical or real crypto+agent application that has NOT been covered in the learning path. For example:
118	- A social media agent that earns tokens for quality content moderation
119	- A healthcare agent that stores patient data on-chain
120	- A creative agent that registers AI-generated art as NFTs and manages licensing royalties
121	
122	Apply the 5-Questions Framework:
123	
124	1. **What agent capability does crypto enable here?**
125	2. **What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?**
126	3. **What new risks does crypto introduce?**
127	4. **Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?**
128	5. **What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?**
129	
130	### Expected Output
131	
132	A structured document (400-500 words) that:
133	- Names and describes the application being evaluated
134	- Answers each of the 5 questions with specific reasoning
135	- Includes a final verdict: "Genuine crypto utility" / "Mixed utility" / "Unnecessary crypto integration"
136	
137	### Verification Check
138	- Did you answer all 5 questions?
139	- Is the verdict supported by the answers to the preceding questions?
140	- Did you consider scaling implications (question 5)?
141	
142	---
143	
144	## Exercise 5: Identify Misconceptions in Crypto+Agent Claims
145	
146	**Stage:** 4 (capstone)
147	**Time:** 30-45 minutes
148	**Format:** Correction exercise
149	
150	### Instructions
151	
152	Review the following claims (or find 3 claims from crypto+agent articles, social posts, or project documentation online). For each claim:
153	
154	1. State whether the claim is **correct**, **partially correct**, or **misleading**
155	2. If misleading or partially correct, explain why in 2-3 sentences
156	3. Suggest a more accurate framing
157	
158	### Claims to Analyze
159	
160	1. "AI Agents will make cryptocurrency obsolete by replacing smart contracts with AI reasoning."
161	2. "Crypto wallets are just like bank accounts for agents."
162	3. "Any AI Agent can automatically interact with any blockchain without additional infrastructure."
163	4. "The main value of crypto for agents is faster payments."
164	5. "Once AI Agents are mainstream, crypto will be unnecessary because AI can handle all coordination."
165	
166	### Expected Output
167	
168	A table or list with 5 claims, each labeled and corrected.
169	
170	### Verification Check
171	- Did you correctly identify at least 3 claims as misleading or partially correct?
172	- Are your corrections specific and grounded in concepts from the learning path?
173	- Do corrections reference specific crypto functions or agent capabilities?
174	
175	---
176	
177	## Exercise Summary
178	
179	| # | Exercise | Stage | Time | Output |
180	|---|----------|-------|------|--------|
181	| 1 | Decompose an AI Agent | 1 | 30-45min | Written decomposition |
182	| 2 | Map Crypto to Agent Needs | 2 | 45-60min | Comparison table |
183	| 3 | Analyze a Real Project | 3 | 60-90min | Project brief |
184	| 4 | Apply 5-Questions Framework | 4 | 45-60min | Evaluation document |
185	| 5 | Identify Misconceptions | 4 | 30-45min | Correction table |

1	# Checkpoints: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era
2	
3	Checkpoints are verifiable milestones that confirm genuine understanding, not just familiarity with terms. Each checkpoint requires active demonstration, not passive recognition.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Checkpoint 1: AI Agent Architecture and Crypto Core Functions
8	
9	**Domain:** Stage 1
10	**Type:** Oral or written explanation
11	**Evaluator:** Self or peer
12	
13	### Task
14	
15	Explain the following to a non-technical person in 3-5 minutes:
16	
17	1. What an AI Agent is (using the 4-component model: Perception, Reasoning, Action, Environment)
18	2. What cryptocurrency's 5 core functions are (on-chain assets, wallets, payment network, smart contracts, staking)
19	3. Why these two systems were historically separate and what the "integration gap" is
20	
21	### Pass Criteria
22	
23	- [ ] Explains all 4 agent components with at least one concrete example for each
24	- [ ] Lists all 5 crypto functions correctly
25	- [ ] Articulates the integration gap without using technical jargon
26	- [ ] Uses no more than 2 minutes on agent architecture and 2 minutes on crypto functions
27	- [ ] Answers one follow-up question about either component correctly
28	
29	### Fail Indicators
30	
31	- Skips or conflates agent components (e.g., "the AI does everything")
32	- Lists fewer than 5 crypto functions or includes non-crypto functions
33	- Cannot explain why agents cannot natively "understand" blockchain
34	- Uses jargon without explanation when speaking to non-technical audience
35	
36	---
37	
38	## Checkpoint 2: Crypto Roles for Agents
39	
40	**Domain:** Stage 2
41	**Type:** Written or diagram-based demonstration
42	**Evaluator:** Self-check against criteria
43	
44	### Task
45	
46	Complete the following task without referring to source materials:
47	
48	Draw or describe a scenario where a single AI Agent uses at least 3 different crypto functions simultaneously. For each function used, specify:
49	- Which crypto function is being used
50	- What problem it solves for the agent
51	- What the agent would do if that crypto function were unavailable
52	
53	### Pass Criteria
54	
55	- [ ] Describes a scenario with at least 3 distinct crypto functions
56	- [ ] Each function is correctly identified and matched to a genuine agent need
57	- [ ] For each function, the alternative (without crypto) is specific, not generic
58	- [ ] The scenario is coherent and internally consistent
59	- [ ] Storage/memory and coordination roles are not confused with payment
60	
61	### Fail Indicators
62	
63	- Describes a scenario that only uses crypto as payment
64	- Uses fewer than 3 crypto functions
65	- The "without crypto" alternative is vague ("the agent would just do it differently")
66	- Confuses blockchain storage with traditional database storage without explanation
67	
68	---
69	
70	## Checkpoint 3: Crypto+Agent Project Evaluation
71	
72	**Domain:** Stage 3
73	**Type:** Structured writing
74	**Evaluator:** Self or peer
75	
76	### Task
77	
78	Given a crypto+agent project description (provided by evaluator or from a public source), write a 200-word evaluation that includes:
79	
80	1. Which of the 5 crypto functions the project primarily uses
81	2. Which agent capability the project enables
82	3. One genuine strength and one unresolved problem
83	4. A 1-sentence verdict on whether the project represents genuine crypto utility or incidental integration
84	
85	### Pass Criteria
86	
87	- [ ] Correctly identifies the primary crypto function
88	- [ ] Correctly links it to an agent capability
89	- [ ] The strength is specific and derived from the project's approach
90	- [ ] The problem is specific (not just "early stage" or "needs more users")
91	- [ ] The verdict follows logically from the preceding analysis
92	- [ ] Entire evaluation is 150-250 words (not under 150 or over 300)
93	
94	### Fail Indicators
95	
96	- Misidentifies the primary crypto function (e.g., calls everything "payment")
97	- Lists multiple strengths or problems instead of selecting the most significant one
98	- The verdict contradicts the preceding analysis
99	- Evaluation is under 150 words (suggests insufficient depth) or over 300 words (suggests lack of precision)
100	
101	---
102	
103	## Checkpoint 4: The 5-Questions Framework
104	
105	**Domain:** Stage 4
106	**Type:** Structured writing or presentation
107	**Evaluator:** Self or peer
108	
109	### Task
110	
111	Take a crypto+agent concept (provided or self-selected) and answer all 5 questions in writing:
112	
113	1. What agent capability does crypto enable here?
114	2. What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?
115	3. What new risks does crypto introduce?
116	4. Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?
117	5. What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?
118	
119	Then write a final verdict: **Genuine crypto utility** / **Mixed utility** / **Unnecessary crypto integration**
120	
121	### Pass Criteria
122	
123	- [ ] Question 1: Correctly identifies the specific crypto function and agent capability
124	- [ ] Question 2: Answer is specific about what breaks, not just "it would be slower" or "it wouldn't work"
125	- [ ] Question 3: Identifies real risks (security, regulatory, UX, liquidity, etc.) not generic ones
126	- [ ] Question 4: Justification is clear and based on questions 1-3
127	- [ ] Question 5: Addresses scaling implications for economics, infrastructure, or coordination
128	- [ ] Verdict matches the reasoning in questions 1-5
129	- [ ] All 5 questions answered with substantive content (not 1 sentence each)
130	
131	### Fail Indicators
132	
133	- Skips orж•·иЎҚ (perfunctorily answers) any of the 5 questions
134	- Question 2 answer is not specific (e.g., "it would be centralized")
135	- Question 4 answer does not connect to the preceding questions
136	- Verdict contradicts the body of the analysis
137	- Answers are so brief they suggest memorization rather than understanding
138	
139	---
140	
141	## Checkpoint Summary Table
142	
143	| # | Checkpoint | Domain | Format | Pass Threshold |
144	|---|------------|--------|--------|----------------|
145	| 1 | Agent Architecture + Crypto Functions | Stage 1 | Oral explanation | 5/5 criteria |
146	| 2 | Multi-function Crypto+Agent Scenario | Stage 2 | Written scenario | 5/5 criteria |
147	| 3 | Project Evaluation | Stage 3 | Structured writing | 6/6 criteria |
148	| 4 | 5-Questions Framework | Stage 4 | Structured writing | 7/7 criteria |
149	
150	---
151	
152	## How to Use These Checkpoints
153	
154	### For Self-Assessment
155	
156	1. Cover the pass criteria before starting
157	2. Complete the task without looking at the criteria
158	3. Check your work against the criteria honestly
159	4. If you fail any criterion, review the relevant stage before proceeding
160	
161	### For Peer Assessment
162	
163	1. Peer evaluator reads the pass criteria (but not to the person being evaluated)
164	2. The evaluated person completes the task
165	3. Peer evaluator checks each criterion and provides specific feedback on failures
166	4. Focus feedback on which specific criterion was not met, not global impressions
167	
168	### Before Proceeding to Next Stage
169	
170	Each checkpoint must be passed (all criteria met) before advancing to the next stage. If you cannot pass a checkpoint, revisit the learning content for that stage and try again after 24 hours.
171

input.content:
# Application Plan: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era

This plan helps you turn understanding of crypto+agent into concrete actions, evaluations, and small deliverables you can complete within days.

---

## Minimum Viable Experiment (Today / This Week)

### Experiment: "Crypto+Agent Use-Case Scanner"

**Time:** 45-60 minutes
**Output:** A structured table evaluating 5 real-world scenarios

**Steps:**

1. **List 5 scenarios** where AI agents interact with value or coordination:
   - A personal agent that pays for API usage
   - A research agent that earns tokens for data provision
   - A creative agent that registers outputs as on-chain assets
   - A coordination agent that stakes tokens to participate in governance
   - A data agent that stores analysis results on decentralized storage

2. **For each scenario, answer:**
   - Which crypto function does this use? (payment / incentive / identity / storage / coordination)
   - Which agent capability does it extend?
   - Is crypto necessary here, or could a traditional system work?

3. **Classify each scenario:**
   - Genuine crypto utility
   - Mixed utility (crypto helps but not essential)
   - Unnecessary crypto integration

**Verification:** You produce a 5-row table with a classification for each scenario and a one-sentence justification.

---

## Action Checklist: From Understanding to Application

### Immediate Actions (This Week)

- [ ] **Complete the minimum experiment above** вҖ” builds intuition through active classification
- [ ] **Read one real project documentation** from the ecosystem (e.g., a protocol that lets agents interact with DeFi, or a decentralized AI compute network)
- [ ] **Write 3 tweets or forum posts** explaining a crypto+agent concept to a non-technical audience, using an analogy (e.g., "crypto for agents is like a bank account + reputation system + automated contract all in one")

### Short-Term Actions (This Month)

- [ ] **Build a one-page evaluation** of a crypto+agent project using the 5-Questions Framework:
  1. What agent capability does crypto enable here?
  2. What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?
  3. What new risks does crypto introduce?
  4. Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?
  5. What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?
- [ ] **Identify one gap or unsolved problem** in current crypto+agent infrastructure and write a one-paragraph description of why it matters
- [ ] **Create a comparison table** of at least 3 crypto+agent projects, categorized by primary crypto function used

### Medium-Term Actions (This Quarter)

- [ ] **Design a hypothetical crypto+agent application** from scratch using the evaluation matrix вҖ” include which crypto functions it uses, what problems they solve, and what tradeoffs exist
- [ ] **Produce a 5-minute explainer** (written or recorded) aimed at a non-technical audience, covering: what an AI agent is, what cryptocurrency does for agents, and one concrete example
- [ ] **Evaluate a real investment or project decision** related to crypto+agent using the framework вҖ” document your reasoning and conclusion

---

## Explanation Framework for Non-Technical Audiences

### The One-Sentence Framing

> "Cryptocurrency gives AI agents something they could never have before: their own bank account, reputation system, and automated contracts вҖ” all built in."

### Three-Column Explanation Table

| Concept | Crypto Analogy | Agent Application |
|---|---|---|
| AI Agent doing work | A freelancer with a skill set | Receives requests, processes them, produces outputs |
| Crypto wallet | A bank account + ID card combined | Agent's identity and ownership of assets |
| Payment layer | Automatic freelance payment | Agent receives compensation when it completes a task |
| Incentive layer | Performance bonus | Agent earns extra tokens for high-quality work |
| Identity layer | Reputation score from past jobs | On-chain history shows if agent is trustworthy |
| Storage layer | Cloud storage + receipt | Agent remembers things permanently, with proof |
| Coordination layer | Airbnb-style smart contract | Two agents agree on a task and payment; no human needed |

### Analogy: Agents as Freelancers with Crypto

Think of an AI agent as a **freelancer** that works 24/7:

- **Without crypto**: The freelancer has no bank account, no reputation, and every payment requires a human to approve and wire money
- **With crypto**: The freelancer has its own bank account (wallet), a reputation based on past work (on-chain history), and automatic payments (smart contracts)

This is why the "agent era" and "crypto era" fit together вҖ” agents need programmable value, and cryptocurrency is programmable value infrastructure.

---

## Decision Checklist: Is Crypto Necessary?

Use this checklist when evaluating any crypto+agent claim or project:

1. **Does this application involve value exchange or financial incentives?** If no, crypto may be incidental.
2. **Does it require trustless execution (no human gatekeeper)?** If yes, smart contracts are relevant.
3. **Does it need verifiable identity tied to history?** If yes, on-chain identity is relevant.
4. **Does it need persistent state that outlasts any single server or company?** If yes, decentralized storage is relevant.
5. **Does it require coordination between agents that do not trust each other?** If yes, crypto coordination layers are relevant.

If 3 or more answers are "yes," crypto is likely a genuine enabler. If fewer than 3, crypto may be added complexity without proportional benefit.

---

## Deliverable Templates

### Template A: One-Pager Project Evaluation

```
# Crypto+Agent Project Evaluation: [Project Name]

## What it does (2-3 sentences)
[Description]

## Primary crypto function used
[Payment / Incentive / Identity / Storage / Coordination]

## Agent capability enabled
[Description]

## What would break without crypto
[Specific description of what breaks]

## Top risk introduced
[Specific risk, not just "early stage"]

## Verdict
[Genuine crypto utility / Mixed utility / Unnecessary integration]
```

### Template B: Five-Minute Explainer Outline

```
# 5-Minute Explainer: Crypto for AI Agents

## Minute 1-2: What is an AI Agent?
- 4-component model (Perception, Reasoning, Action, Environment)
- Concrete example: a research agent that finds articles, summarizes them, and delivers the summary

## Minute 2-3: What does crypto add?
- The problem: agents need to get paid, prove identity, store memory, coordinate
- The crypto solution: wallet = bank account + ID, smart contract = automatic agreement, on-chain storage = permanent memory

## Minute 3-4: A real example
- Name a specific project and what it does
- Which crypto functions it uses
- What agent capability it enables

## Minute 4-5: Why it matters
- The opportunity: agents as independent economic actors
- The risk: early stage, experimental, rapidly changing
- Key takeaway: crypto is the infrastructure layer; agents are the interface layer
```

---

## Key Takeaway

Crypto does not make agents "better" in the sense of better reasoning or better language understanding. Instead, crypto makes agents **economically autonomous** вҖ” capable of receiving value, providing guarantees, building reputation, and coordinating without human intermediaries. Understanding this distinction is the foundation for all further application of this knowledge.

# Transfer Plan: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era

This plan helps you transfer what you have learned to others: explain it clearly, apply it to new situations, and build on it independently.

---

## How to Teach This to Peers or Non-Technical Audiences

### Core Transfer Principle

When teaching crypto+agent concepts, **always start with what the audience already understands** (payments, identities, contracts) before introducing the new concepts (autonomous agents, on-chain identity, trustless coordination).

### Recommended Teaching Sequence

**Step 1: Establish the Agent Concept (5 minutes)**

Do not start with "AI Agent" as a technical term. Start with a concrete scenario:

> "Imagine a personal assistant that watches your calendar, reads your emails, makes decisions on its own, and takes actions вҖ” like booking flights or sending invoices вҖ” without asking you every time. That is an AI agent."

Then introduce the 4-component model briefly:

- **Perception**: What the agent sees (inputs)
- **Reasoning**: What the agent decides
- **Action**: What the agent does
- **Environment**: What systems the agent interacts with

**Step 2: Introduce the Problem (3 minutes)**

Ask the audience: "If this agent is going to work autonomously, what does it need?"

Guide them toward:

- It needs its own way to receive money
- It needs a way to prove who it is
- It needs a way to remember things permanently
- It needs a way to make agreements with other agents

These are exactly the functions that cryptocurrency provides.

**Step 3: Map Crypto to Agent Needs (5 minutes)**

Use the three-column explanation table from the application plan:

| Agent Need | Crypto Function | Simple Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Get paid | Payment network | "Its own bank account" |
| Prove identity | Wallet + on-chain history | "Its work resume and ID" |
| Remember things | On-chain storage | "Its permanent memory" |
| Make agreements | Smart contracts | "Its automatic contracts" |

**Step 4: Give One Concrete Example (5 minutes)**

Use the Autonomous Economic Agent (AEA) example:

> "A research agent earns tokens by finding and summarizing articles. It gets paid automatically via smart contract when it delivers the summary. Its wallet address becomes its identity вҖ” the more quality summaries it delivers, the higher its on-chain reputation. It can use those tokens to pay for better computing resources or stake them to participate in governance."

**Step 5: Open Discussion (5-10 minutes)**

Ask the audience:

- "What do you think are the risks of letting agents manage money on their own?"
- "Would you trust an agent with your finances? Why or why not?"
- "What happens if the agent makes a mistake вҖ” who is responsible?"

These questions reinforce learning and surface misconceptions.

---

## How to Adapt to New Situations

### Applying the Framework to a New Crypto+Agent Concept

When you encounter a new project, claim, or use case:

1. **Identify the agent type**: What does this agent perceive, reason about, and act on?
2. **Identify the crypto function**: Which of the 5 functions does it use? (payment, incentive, identity, storage, coordination)
3. **Ask the 5 Questions**:
   - What agent capability does crypto enable here?
   - What would break without crypto?
   - What new risks does crypto introduce?
   - Is crypto necessary or incidental?
   - What happens at scale?
4. **Form a verdict**: Genuine utility / Mixed utility / Unnecessary integration

### Scenario Adaptation Table

| New Situation | What to Do |
|---|---|
| You read a new crypto+agent project announcement | Apply the 5-Questions Framework to it |
| Someone asks you "what can crypto do for AI agents?" | Use the 3-column table and AEA example |
| You evaluate whether a project genuinely needs crypto | Use the Decision Checklist (3+ yes = genuine utility) |
| You encounter a claim that "AI will replace crypto" | Identify which part of the stack each technology operates on |
| You see a crypto+agent project that only uses payment | Ask whether storage, identity, or coordination would add more value |

---

## Minimum Next Actions for Independent Mastery

### Immediate Next Step (Today)

**Write your own 5-minute explainer.** Take one specific crypto+agent concept (from the learning path, a project you researched, or a new one) and write a 300-word explanation aimed at a friend who knows nothing about agents or crypto. Read it out loud вҖ” if it sounds natural and clear, you understand it well.

### This Week

**Complete the "Crypto+Agent Use-Case Scanner"** from the application plan. This exercises your ability to classify scenarios, which is the core analytical skill for this domain.

### This Month

**Build a personal reference document** summarizing:

- Your own definition of each of the 5 crypto functions in agent context (in 1-2 sentences each)
- Your own analogy for each function (refined for your own mental model)
- Your own one-paragraph evaluation of the most interesting crypto+agent project you have encountered

This document becomes your mental toolkit for future reasoning.

### This Quarter

**Produce one substantial output** from one of these options:

- A blog post (800-1200 words) on a crypto+agent topic you care about
- A 10-slide presentation for a technical or semi-technical audience
- A comparison analysis of 2 crypto+agent projects using the evaluation framework
- A design document for a hypothetical crypto+agent application you would build

---

## Common Transfer Pitfalls

| Pitfall | Why It Happens | How to Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| Jumping straight to technical details | Enthusiasm about the technology | Always anchor to agent needs first, then show how crypto fills them |
| Conflating payment with the whole story | Payment is the most familiar crypto concept | Explicitly name all 5 functions and give non-payment examples |
| Assuming the audience knows what an AI agent is | Technical audiences may have different mental models | Start with the 4-component model and concrete example |
| Overclaiming for crypto | Crypto enthusiasm can lead to overstatement | Apply the "necessary or incidental" question rigorously |
| Underclaiming for crypto | Skepticism can lead to underselling the potential | Focus on what breaks without crypto, not just what crypto adds |

---

## Verification: Have You Truly Transferred This Knowledge?

You have transferred this knowledge when you can:

1. **Explain the crypto+agent intersection** to a non-technical friend in under 5 minutes without using jargon
2. **Classify a new project** in under 10 minutes using the 5-Questions Framework
3. **Identify a misconception** in a crypto+agent claim and explain why it is wrong using concepts from the learning path
4. **Design a new hypothetical application** that uses at least 2 crypto functions for an agent, with honest tradeoffs identified
5. **Teach the 4-component agent model** and the 5 crypto functions to someone else and have them correctly explain it back

If you can do all 5, you have moved from understanding to genuine mastery.

61	7. дёҚеј•е…Ҙиҫ“е…ҘдёӯжңӘжҸҗдҫӣзҡ„дёӘдәәгҖҒиЎҢдёҡгҖҒиҒҢдёҡжҲ–дә§е“ҒиғҢжҷҜгҖӮ

1	# Application Plan: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era
2	
3	This plan helps you turn understanding of crypto+agent into concrete actions, evaluations, and small deliverables you can complete within days.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Minimum Viable Experiment (Today / This Week)
8	
9	### Experiment: "Crypto+Agent Use-Case Scanner"
10	
11	**Time:** 45-60 minutes
12	**Output:** A structured table evaluating 5 real-world scenarios
13	
14	**Steps:**
15	
16	1. **List 5 scenarios** where AI agents interact with value or coordination:
17	   - A personal agent that pays for API usage
18	   - A research agent that earns tokens for data provision
19	   - A creative agent that registers outputs as on-chain assets
20	   - A coordination agent that stakes tokens to participate in governance
21	   - A data agent that stores analysis results on decentralized storage
22	
23	2. **For each scenario, answer:**
24	   - Which crypto function does this use? (payment / incentive / identity / storage / coordination)
25	   - Which agent capability does it extend?
26	   - Is crypto necessary here, or could a traditional system work?
27	
28	3. **Classify each scenario:**
29	   - Genuine crypto utility
30	   - Mixed utility (crypto helps but not essential)
31	   - Unnecessary crypto integration
32	
33	**Verification:** You produce a 5-row table with a classification for each scenario and a one-sentence justification.
34	
35	---
36	
37	## Action Checklist: From Understanding to Application
38	
39	### Immediate Actions (This Week)
40	
41	- [ ] **Complete the minimum experiment above** вҖ” builds intuition through active classification
42	- [ ] **Read one real project documentation** from the ecosystem (e.g., a protocol that lets agents interact with DeFi, or a decentralized AI compute network)
43	- [ ] **Write 3 tweets or forum posts** explaining a crypto+agent concept to a non-technical audience, using an analogy (e.g., "crypto for agents is like a bank account + reputation system + automated contract all in one")
44	
45	### Short-Term Actions (This Month)
46	
47	- [ ] **Build a one-page evaluation** of a crypto+agent project using the 5-Questions Framework:
48	  1. What agent capability does crypto enable here?
49	  2. What would break if crypto were replaced by traditional systems?
50	  3. What new risks does crypto introduce?
51	  4. Is the crypto component necessary or incidental?
52	  5. What does this look like when scaled to millions of agents?
53	- [ ] **Identify one gap or unsolved problem** in current crypto+agent infrastructure and write a one-paragraph description of why it matters
54	- [ ] **Create a comparison table** of at least 3 crypto+agent projects, categorized by primary crypto function used
55	
56	### Medium-Term Actions (This Quarter)
57	
58	- [ ] **Design a hypothetical crypto+agent application** from scratch using the evaluation matrix вҖ” include which crypto functions it uses, what problems they solve, and what tradeoffs exist
59	- [ ] **Produce a 5-minute explainer** (written or recorded) aimed at a non-technical audience, covering: what an AI agent is, what cryptocurrency does for agents, and one concrete example
60	- [ ] **Evaluate a real investment or project decision** related to crypto+agent using the framework вҖ” document your reasoning and conclusion
61	
62	---
63	
64	## Explanation Framework for Non-Technical Audiences
65	
66	### The One-Sentence Framing
67	
68	> "Cryptocurrency gives AI agents something they could never have before: their own bank account, reputation system, and automated contracts вҖ” all built in."
69	
70	### Three-Column Explanation Table
71	
72	| Concept | Crypto Analogy | Agent Application |
73	|---|---|---|
74	| AI Agent doing work | A freelancer with a skill set | Receives requests, processes them, produces outputs |
75	| Crypto wallet | A bank account + ID card combined | Agent's identity and ownership of assets |
76	| Payment layer | Automatic freelance payment | Agent receives compensation when it completes a task |
77	| Incentive layer | Performance bonus | Agent earns extra tokens for high-quality work |
78	| Identity layer | Reputation score from past jobs | On-chain history shows if agent is trustworthy |
79	| Storage layer | Cloud storage + receipt | Agent remembers things permanently, with proof |
80	| Coordination layer | Airbnb-style smart contract | Two agents agree on a task and payment; no human needed |
81	
82	### Analogy: Agents as Freelancers with Crypto
83	
84	Think of an AI agent as a **freelancer** that works 24/7:
85	
86	- **Without crypto**: The freelancer has no bank account, no reputation, and every payment requires a human to approve and wire money
87	- **With crypto**: The freelancer has its own bank account (wallet), a reputation based on past work (on-chain history), and automatic payments (smart contracts)
88	
89	This is why the "agent era" and "crypto era" fit together вҖ” agents need programmable value, and cryptocurrency is programmable value infrastructure.
90	
91	---
92	
93	## Decision Checklist: Is Crypto Necessary?
94	
95	Use this checklist when evaluating any crypto+agent claim or project:
96	
97	1. **Does this application involve value exchange or financial incentives?** If no, crypto may be incidental.
98	2. **Does it require trustless execution (no human gatekeeper)?** If yes, smart contracts are relevant.
99	3. **Does it need verifiable identity tied to history?** If yes, on-chain identity is relevant.
100	4. **Does it need persistent state that outlasts any single server or company?** If yes, decentralized storage is relevant.
101	5. **Does it require coordination between agents that do not trust each other?** If yes, crypto coordination layers are relevant.
102	
103	If 3 or more answers are "yes," crypto is likely a genuine enabler. If fewer than 3, crypto may be added complexity without proportional benefit.
104	
105	---
106	
107	## Deliverable Templates
108	
109	### Template A: One-Pager Project Evaluation
110	
111	```
112	# Crypto+Agent Project Evaluation: [Project Name]
113	
114	## What it does (2-3 sentences)
115	[Description]
116	
117	## Primary crypto function used
118	[Payment / Incentive / Identity / Storage / Coordination]
119	
120	## Agent capability enabled
121	[Description]
122	
123	## What would break without crypto
124	[Specific description of what breaks]
125	
126	## Top risk introduced
127	[Specific risk, not just "early stage"]
128	
129	## Verdict
130	[Genuine crypto utility / Mixed utility / Unnecessary integration]
131	```
132	
133	### Template B: Five-Minute Explainer Outline
134	
135	```
136	# 5-Minute Explainer: Crypto for AI Agents
137	
138	## Minute 1-2: What is an AI Agent?
139	- 4-component model (Perception, Reasoning, Action, Environment)
140	- Concrete example: a research agent that finds articles, summarizes them, and delivers the summary
141	
142	## Minute 2-3: What does crypto add?
143	- The problem: agents need to get paid, prove identity, store memory, coordinate
144	- The crypto solution: wallet = bank account + ID, smart contract = automatic agreement, on-chain storage = permanent memory
145	
146	## Minute 3-4: A real example
147	- Name a specific project and what it does
148	- Which crypto functions it uses
149	- What agent capability it enables
150	
151	## Minute 4-5: Why it matters
152	- The opportunity: agents as independent economic actors
153	- The risk: early stage, experimental, rapidly changing
154	- Key takeaway: crypto is the infrastructure layer; agents are the interface layer
155	```
156	
157	---
158	
159	## Key Takeaway
160	
161	Crypto does not make agents "better" in the sense of better reasoning or better language understanding. Instead, crypto makes agents **economically autonomous** вҖ” capable of receiving value, providing guarantees, building reputation, and coordinating without human intermediaries. Understanding this distinction is the foundation for all further application of this knowledge.
162

1	# Transfer Plan: Cryptocurrency in the Agent Era
2	
3	This plan helps you transfer what you have learned to others: explain it clearly, apply it to new situations, and build on it independently.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## How to Teach This to Peers or Non-Technical Audiences
8	
9	### Core Transfer Principle
10	
11	When teaching crypto+agent concepts, **always start with what the audience already understands** (payments, identities, contracts) before introducing the new concepts (autonomous agents, on-chain identity, trustless coordination).
12	
13	### Recommended Teaching Sequence
14	
15	**Step 1: Establish the Agent Concept (5 minutes)**
16	
17	Do not start with "AI Agent" as a technical term. Start with a concrete scenario:
18	
19	> "Imagine a personal assistant that watches your calendar, reads your emails, makes decisions on its own, and takes actions вҖ” like booking flights or sending invoices вҖ” without asking you every time. That is an AI agent."
20	
21	Then introduce the 4-component model briefly:
22	
23	- **Perception**: What the agent sees (inputs)
24	- **Reasoning**: What the agent decides
25	- **Action**: What the agent does
26	- **Environment**: What systems the agent interacts with
27	
28	**Step 2: Introduce the Problem (3 minutes)**
29	
30	Ask the audience: "If this agent is going to work autonomously, what does it need?"
31	
32	Guide them toward:
33	
34	- It needs its own way to receive money
35	- It needs a way to prove who it is
36	- It needs a way to remember things permanently
37	- It needs a way to make agreements with other agents
38	
39	These are exactly the functions that cryptocurrency provides.
40	
41	**Step 3: Map Crypto to Agent Needs (5 minutes)**
42	
43	Use the three-column explanation table from the application plan:
44	
45	| Agent Need | Crypto Function | Simple Analogy |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| Get paid | Payment network | "Its own bank account" |
48	| Prove identity | Wallet + on-chain history | "Its work resume and ID" |
49	| Remember things | On-chain storage | "Its permanent memory" |
50	| Make agreements | Smart contracts | "Its automatic contracts" |
51	
52	**Step 4: Give One Concrete Example (5 minutes)**
53	
54	Use the Autonomous Economic Agent (AEA) example:
55	
56	> "A research agent earns tokens by finding and summarizing articles. It gets paid automatically via smart contract when it delivers the summary. Its wallet address becomes its identity вҖ” the more quality summaries it delivers, the higher its on-chain reputation. It can use those tokens to pay for better computing resources or stake them to participate in governance."
57	
58	**Step 5: Open Discussion (5-10 minutes)**
59	
60	Ask the audience:
61	
62	- "What do you think are the risks of letting agents manage money on their own?"
63	- "Would you trust an agent with your finances? Why or why not?"
64	- "What happens if the agent makes a mistake вҖ” who is responsible?"
65	
66	These questions reinforce learning and surface misconceptions.
67	
68	---
69	
70	## How to Adapt to New Situations
71	
72	### Applying the Framework to a New Crypto+Agent Concept
73	
74	When you encounter a new project, claim, or use case:
75	
76	1. **Identify the agent type**: What does this agent perceive, reason about, and act on?
77	2. **Identify the crypto function**: Which of the 5 functions does it use? (payment, incentive, identity, storage, coordination)
78	3. **Ask the 5 Questions**:
79	   - What agent capability does crypto enable here?
80	   - What would break without crypto?
81	   - What new risks does crypto introduce?
82	   - Is crypto necessary or incidental?
83	   - What happens at scale?
84	4. **Form a verdict**: Genuine utility / Mixed utility / Unnecessary integration
85	
86	### Scenario Adaptation Table
87	
88	| New Situation | What to Do |
89	|---|---|
90	| You read a new crypto+agent project announcement | Apply the 5-Questions Framework to it |
91	| Someone asks you "what can crypto do for AI agents?" | Use the 3-column table and AEA example |
92	| You evaluate whether a project genuinely needs crypto | Use the Decision Checklist (3+ yes = genuine utility) |
93	| You encounter a claim that "AI will replace crypto" | Identify which part of the stack each technology operates on |
94	| You see a crypto+agent project that only uses payment | Ask whether storage, identity, or coordination would add more value |
95	
96	---
97	
98	## Minimum Next Actions for Independent Mastery
99	
100	### Immediate Next Step (Today)
101	
102	**Write your own 5-minute explainer.** Take one specific crypto+agent concept (from the learning path, a project you researched, or a new one) and write a 300-word explanation aimed at a friend who knows nothing about agents or crypto. Read it out loud вҖ” if it sounds natural and clear, you understand it well.
103	
104	### This Week
105	
106	**Complete the "Crypto+Agent Use-Case Scanner"** from the application plan. This exercises your ability to classify scenarios, which is the core analytical skill for this domain.
107	
108	### This Month
109	
110	**Build a personal reference document** summarizing:
111	
112	- Your own definition of each of the 5 crypto functions in agent context (in 1-2 sentences each)
113	- Your own analogy for each function (refined for your own mental model)
114	- Your own one-paragraph evaluation of the most interesting crypto+agent project you have encountered
115	
116	This document becomes your mental toolkit for future reasoning.
117	
118	### This Quarter
119	
120	**Produce one substantial output** from one of these options:
121	
122	- A blog post (800-1200 words) on a crypto+agent topic you care about
123	- A 10-slide presentation for a technical or semi-technical audience
124	- A comparison analysis of 2 crypto+agent projects using the evaluation framework
125	- A design document for a hypothetical crypto+agent application you would build
126	
127	---
128	
129	## Common Transfer Pitfalls
130	
131	| Pitfall | Why It Happens | How to Avoid |
132	|---|---|---|
133	| Jumping straight to technical details | Enthusiasm about the technology | Always anchor to agent needs first, then show how crypto fills them |
134	| Conflating payment with the whole story | Payment is the most familiar crypto concept | Explicitly name all 5 functions and give non-payment examples |
135	| Assuming the audience knows what an AI agent is | Technical audiences may have different mental models | Start with the 4-component model and concrete example |
136	| Overclaiming for crypto | Crypto enthusiasm can lead to overstatement | Apply the "necessary or incidental" question rigorously |
137	| Underclaiming for crypto | Skepticism can lead to underselling the potential | Focus on what breaks without crypto, not just what crypto adds |
138	
139	---
140	
141	## Verification: Have You Truly Transferred This Knowledge?
142	
143	You have transferred this knowledge when you can:
144	
145	1. **Explain the crypto+agent intersection** to a non-technical friend in under 5 minutes without using jargon
146	2. **Classify a new project** in under 10 minutes using the 5-Questions Framework
147	3. **Identify a misconception** in a crypto+agent claim and explain why it is wrong using concepts from the learning path
148	4. **Design a new hypothetical application** that uses at least 2 crypto functions for an agent, with honest tradeoffs identified
149	5. **Teach the 4-component agent model** and the 5 crypto functions to someone else and have them correctly explain it back
150	
151	If you can do all 5, you have moved from understanding to genuine mastery.
152

35

## иҜ„дј°иҜҙжҳҺ

**Question Quality**пјҡapplication-plan.md еҮҶзЎ®йҮҚиҝ°еҺҹе§Ӣй—®йўҳпјҲеҠ еҜҶиҙ§еёҒеңЁagentж—¶д»Јзҡ„дҪңз”ЁпјүпјҢжҳҺзЎ®"turn understanding into concrete actions, evaluations, and small deliverables"гҖӮtransfer-plan.md йҮҚиҝ°дёә"transfer what you have learned to others: explain it clearly, apply it to new situations, and build on it independently"гҖӮеӯҰд№ зӣ®ж ҮжҳҺзЎ®пјҡеә”з”Ёи®ЎеҲ’е°ҶзҹҘиҜҶиҪ¬еҢ–дёәиЎҢеҠЁпјҢиҝҒз§»и®ЎеҲ’е°ҶзҹҘиҜҶиҪ¬еҢ–дёәеҸҜж•ҷжҺҲгҖҒеҸҜиҝҒз§»зҡ„иғҪеҠӣгҖӮиҫ№з•Ңжё…жҷ°пјҢдёҺcontractдёӯжҡӮдёҚиҰҶзӣ–зҡ„4дёӘж–№йқўдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ

**Coverage**пјҡapplication-plan.md зҡ„"5-Questions Framework"пјҲжҜҸйЎ№agentиғҪеҠӣгҖҒcryptoеҠҹиғҪгҖҒеҝ…иҰҒжҖ§гҖҒи§„жЁЎпјүзӣҙжҺҘеҜ№еә”domain-map.md Section 6зҡ„5дёӘKey QuestionsгҖӮtransfer-plan.md зҡ„Teaching SequenceдҪҝз”Ё4-component modelпјҲPerception, Reasoning, Action, EnvironmentпјүпјҢдёҺdomain-map.md Section 1.1иЎЁж јдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ3-column explanation tableпјҲAgent Need / Crypto Function / AnalogyпјүиҰҶзӣ–5дёӘcrypto functionsгҖӮдёӨиҖ…еқҮж— йҒ—жјҸдё»е№ІжҲ–еј•е…Ҙиҫ№зјҳиҜқйўҳгҖӮ

**Clarity**пјҡapplication-plan.md зҡ„Minimum Viable ExperimentжӯҘйӘӨжё…жҷ°пјҲList 5 scenarios вҶ’ For each answer 3 sub-questions вҶ’ ClassifyпјүпјҢиҫ“еҮәж јејҸжҳҺзЎ®пјҲ5-row tableпјүгҖӮAction ChecklistжҢүж—¶й—ҙеҲҶеұӮпјҲThis Week / This Month / This QuarterпјүгҖӮDecision Checklistжңү5дёӘе…·дҪ“й—®йўҳпјҢ3+ yes = genuine utilityзҡ„еҲӨж–ӯж ҮеҮҶжё…жҷ°гҖӮtransfer-plan.md зҡ„5жӯҘж•ҷеӯҰеәҸеҲ—жңүзІҫзЎ®ж—¶й—ҙеҲҶй…ҚпјҲ5min / 3min / 5min / 5min / 5-10minпјүгҖӮTransferability Verificationзҡ„5дёӘ"you can when"ж ҮеҮҶе…·дҪ“еҸҜйӘҢиҜҒгҖӮDeliverable Templates Aе’ҢBз»ҷеҮәжҳҺзЎ®ж јејҸжЎҶжһ¶гҖӮ

**Actionability**пјҡapplication-plan.md зҡ„"Crypto+Agent Use-Case Scanner"жҳҜжңҖе°ҸдёӢдёҖжӯҘпјҲ45-60еҲҶй’ҹпјҢеҪ“еӨ©еҸҜе®ҢжҲҗпјүпјҢиҫ“еҮәдёә5иЎҢз»“жһ„еҢ–иЎЁж јпјҢеёҰйӘҢиҜҒж ҮеҮҶпјҲ"You produce a 5-row table with a classification for each scenario and a one-sentence justification"пјүгҖӮThis Week Actionsжңү3дёӘе…·дҪ“йЎ№пјҢThis Monthжңү3дёӘпјҢThis Quarterжңү3дёӘпјҢеқҮдёәеҸҜжү§иЎҢиҫ“еҮәиҖҢйқһз©әиҜқгҖӮtransfer-plan.md зҡ„"Write your own 5-minute explainer"пјҲ300иҜҚпјҢеҪ“еӨ©еҸҜе®ҢжҲҗпјүжҳҜжңҖе°ҸдёӢдёҖжӯҘгҖӮThis Monthзҡ„"Build a personal reference document"жңүе…·дҪ“еҶ…е®№иҰҒжұӮпјҲ5дёӘе®ҡд№үгҖҒ5дёӘзұ»жҜ”гҖҒ1ж®өиҜ„д»·пјүгҖӮThis Quarterзҡ„4дёӘйҖүйЎ№пјҲblog post / 10-slide presentation / comparison analysis / design documentпјүеқҮеҸҜдәӨд»ҳгҖӮ

**User Context Fit**пјҡlearning-contractжҳҺзЎ®еҶҷжҳҺ"жңӘжҢҮе®ҡпјӣжҢүйҖҡз”ЁеӯҰд№ иҖ…еӨ„зҗҶ"гҖӮдёӨж–ҮжЎЈе…ЁзЁӢдҪҝз”ЁйҖҡз”ЁжҰӮеҝөпјҢж— дёӘдәә/иЎҢдёҡ/иҒҢдёҡ/дә§е“ҒиғҢжҷҜгҖӮж•ҷеӯҰзұ»жҜ”дҪҝз”Ё"freelancer"гҖҒ"bank account"гҖҒ"reputation system"зӯүжҷ®йҒҚжҰӮеҝөпјҢж— зү№е®ҡиЎҢдёҡз»‘е®ҡгҖӮAEAдҫӢеӯҗпјҲresearch agent earns tokensпјүжҳҜйҖҡз”ЁеңәжҷҜжҸҸиҝ°пјҢйқһзү№е®ҡдә§е“ҒжҲ–е…¬еҸёгҖӮжүҖжңүcrypto functionsе’Ңagent capabilitiesеқҮдёәйҖҡз”ЁеҹәзЎҖи®ҫж–ҪжҰӮеҝөгҖӮ

**Transferability**пјҡtransfer-plan.md зҡ„Teaching Sequenceдё“дёәеҗ‘еҗҢдјҙжҲ–йқһжҠҖжңҜеҸ—дј—и§ЈйҮҠи®ҫи®ЎпјҢжҜҸжӯҘжңүе…·дҪ“й—®йўҳе’Ңеј•еҜјж–№еҗ‘гҖӮScenario Adaptation TableиҰҶзӣ–5з§Қж–°жғ…еўғпјҲnew project announcement / peer question / project evaluation / "AI will replace crypto" claim / project with only paymentпјүпјҢеқҮжңүе…·дҪ“еә”еҜ№ж–№жі•гҖӮCommon Transfer Pitfalls Tableжңү5дёӘе…·дҪ“йҷ·йҳұгҖҒеҺҹеӣ е’ҢйҒҝе…Қж–№жі•гҖӮVerification: 5дёӘ"You have transferred when"ж ҮеҮҶе…·дҪ“еҸҜйӘҢиҜҒпјҲexplain in under 5 minutes, classify in under 10 minutes, identify misconception, design application, teach backпјүгҖӮapplication-plan.md зҡ„Deliverable TemplatesеҸҜзӣҙжҺҘз”ЁдәҺз”ҹдә§еҶ…е®№гҖӮ

## дә§зү©дёҖиҮҙжҖ§жЈҖжҹҘ

- application-plan.md зҡ„"5-Questions Framework"дёҺdomain-map.md Section 6зҡ„5дёӘKey QuestionsзӣҙжҺҘеҜ№еә”гҖӮ
- transfer-plan.md зҡ„Teaching Sequence Step 1дҪҝз”Ёзҡ„4-component modelдёҺdomain-map.md Section 1.1иЎЁж јдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ
- application-plan.md зҡ„3-column explanation tableпјҲwallet / payment / incentive / identity / storage / coordinationпјүдёҺdomain-map.md Section 1.3зҡ„5 layersдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ
- дёӨж–ҮжЎЈеқҮеј•з”ЁAEAпјҲAutonomous Economic AgentпјүжҰӮеҝөпјҢдёҺdomain-map.md Section 2.1дёҖиҮҙгҖӮ
- дёӨж–ҮжЎЈеқҮж— зҹӣзӣҫжҲ–ж–ӯеұӮгҖӮ

## Task 3 дёҺ Task 1/Task 2 иЎ”жҺҘжЈҖжҹҘ

- Task 3зҡ„application planе°ҶTask 2д№ еҫ—зҡ„жҰӮеҝөпјҲ5 crypto functions, 5-questions frameworkпјүиҪ¬еҢ–дёәе…·дҪ“иЎҢеҠЁгҖӮ

- дёӨж–ҮжЎЈеқҮжңҚеҠЎlearning-contractдёӯзҡ„жңҖз»Ҳзӣ®ж Үпјҡ"еҪўжҲҗеҜ№crypto+agentдәӨеҸүйўҶеҹҹзҡ„зі»з»ҹи®ӨзҹҘжЎҶжһ¶"е№¶иғҪ"еҲӨж–ӯеңЁдёҚеҗҢAgentеә”з”ЁеңәжҷҜдёӯзҡ„и§’иүІ"гҖӮ
